title: Discovery recap + 30min demo – NimbusHealth
preview:
From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Desai <priya.desai@nimbushealth.com>, Sanjay Rao <sanjay.rao@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-08-16T10:12:00-07:00
Subject: Discovery recap + 30min demo – NimbusHealth

Hi Priya and Sanjay,

Thanks for taking the time earlier — solid conversation. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum:

- Problem you emphasized: reduce transcription latency for clinician notes and lower per-record embedding costs for search.
- Priority use case: live-transcription + post-visit summarization for ~1.5k clinicians in pilot.
- Success criteria: <300ms median token latency for generation, 85%+ summary accuracy on our eval prompts, predictable monthly unit costs.

Next step: 30-minute product intro/demo. Below are three options (all PT):

1) Tue Aug 24 — 09:30–10:00 PT
2) Wed Aug 25 — 14:00–14:30 PT
3) Thu Aug 26 — 11:00–11:30 PT

Please reply with the slot that works best and a short list of attendees (who from clinical, infra/security, procurement should join). If none of those work, propose 2–3 alternate windows and we’ll do our best to accommodate.

Pre-demo asks:
- Do you want a brief SOC 2 / data handling overview included? (Yes/No)
- Any specific datasets or PII redaction examples we should prepare?

I’ve attached a short agenda (MVP-demo-agenda.pdf) and a one-pager on performance profiles. Will include Mina (SE) to cover infra/latency questions.

Thanks,

Ethan Park
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwoodinference.com
(415) 555-0183

Attachment: MVP-demo-agenda.pdf (application/pdf, 120KB)

title: EmbeddingOps discovery — BrightCart (index scale, SLA & batching follow-up)
preview:
From: Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.com>
To: Kayla Nguyen <kayla@brightcart.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 12 Aug 2026 11:15:00 -0700
Subject: EmbeddingOps discovery — BrightCart (index scale, SLA & batching follow-up)

Thanks again for taking the time today, Kayla — really helpful kickoff. Quick recap of what I heard and the proposed next steps so we’re aligned:

Key pain points (as discussed)
- Search relevance has regressed over the past 6 months after catalog growth and frequent product copy changes — merch team sees a ~12% drop in clicks on recommended results.
- Current stack (confirmed): Pinecone for vector store + Redis for session caching; embedding model is the 1536-dimension open model running as a batch job.
- Operational pain: nightly full reindex of ~1.8B vectors takes ~10 hours, incremental updates are tricky (latency spikes during peak hours).

Scale & workload signals
- Index size: ~1.8 billion dense vectors, dim=1536 (product titles + metadata embeddings).
- QPS: baseline 120 qps, spikes to 300 qps during promotions.
- Query profile: 80% short real-time queries (1–3 vectors per user interaction), 20% complex multi-stage reranking flows (candidate set 200–500).
- Batch patterns: daily full rebuild (offline) + hourly incremental push (micro-batches ~5k vectors).

SLA & success criteria
- Latency target: p95 <= 120 ms for single-query retrieval + top-200 candidate rerank within 300 ms end-to-end (user-visible).
- Cost target: reduce per-query serving cost by at least 30% vs current spend while improving p95.
- Reliability: avoid user-facing degradation during hourly increments and promotions; graceful fallback to smaller candidate sets if load spikes.

Technical constraints
- Cloud: mostly GCP us-central.
- Security: cannot send raw user PII to third-party indexing service; need SOC2 evidence and ability to run Private deployment.
- Indexing: prefer HNSW-style indexes but open to hybrid ANN + filtered metadata approach.

Immediate next steps (action items)
1) Kayla: share the anonymized sample query set + a small slice of product vectors (attachment suggestion) — we can use this to run a 2-day eval.
2) Priyom: provision a short-lived sandbox (Redwood hosted trial) with a Milvus/FAISS-backed config tuned for dim=1536 and a sample shard layout; will include latency/cost dashboard.
3) Laura: prepare an SLA/Private-deployment checklist and SOC2 evidence sheet; share by EOD Thu.
4) Joint: schedule a technical deep-dive with BrightCart infra and search engineers next week (45–60 mins).

Attachments: please send an anonymized query sample (CSV) and small vector slice (<=100k vectors) — we referenced a CSV template during the call.

If that looks right I’ll queue the sandbox and follow up with calendar options for the deep-dive.

—
Priyom Das
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
pri***@redwood.com
(415) 555-0198

Attachment placeholders: brightcart-query-sample-aug2026.csv (requested)


From: Kayla Nguyen <kayla@brightcart.com>
To: Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.com>
Cc: BrightCart Search Team <search@brightcart.com>
Date: Wed, 12 Aug 2026 14:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: EmbeddingOps discovery — BrightCart (index scale, SLA & batching follow-up)

Thanks, Priyom — this is accurate and helpful. A couple quick clarifications:
- The 1.8B number includes archived SKUs (we can exclude old SKUs if that helps).
- Our rerank currently uses a dense-first candidate retrieval then a small transformer reranker on the top 200. We’d like to keep that pattern if possible.

I attached a tiny sample (1000 rows) and a redacted query set for the eval. Also attaching the internal SLO note for peak windows so you see how strict the ops team is.

Can you confirm whether the sandbox will allow us to test both incremental push and live-replace full rebuild flows? That’s one of the major validation points for us.

—
Kayla Nguyen
Head of Search, BrightCart
kayla@brightcart.com
Attachment: brightcart-query-sample-aug2026.csv
Attachment: brightcart-slo-peak-windows.pdf


From: Priyom Das <pri***@redwood.com>
To: Kayla Nguyen <kayla@brightcart.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 13 Aug 2026 08:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: EmbeddingOps discovery — BrightCart (index scale, SLA & batching follow-up)

Kayla — thanks for sending the samples and the SLO note. Quick responses inline:
- Sandbox capabilities: yes — the trial environment will support both incremental push (streaming micro-batch ingestion, commit/merge semantics) and full replace workflows. We’ll provide a sample ingestion pipeline that mirrors your hourly micro-batch flow so you can validate CPU/GPU contention during pushes.
- Index config we’ll test: HNSW-VPT hybrid for dense retrieval, sharded FAISS for large-scale disk-backed recall, and a candidate cache for the top 10k trending SKUs.

Next steps I’m executing today:
- Spinning up the sandbox cluster (expected available by Fri 08/14, 11:00 PT).
- Laura will send the SOC2 / Private deployment checklist by EOD Thu as promised.
- Sending a short HubSpot note with the proposed technical deep-dive slots (I’ll include a link to a short pre-call checklist for your infra folks).

Proposed deep-dive times: Mon 08/17 09:00–10:00 PT, Tue 08/18 13:00–14:00 PT. Which works better for you?

—
Priyom Das
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Kayla Nguyen <kayla@brightcart.com>
To: Priyom Das <priom@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 13 Aug 2026 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: EmbeddingOps discovery — BrightCart (index scale, SLA & batching follow-up)

Mon 08/17 09:00 PT works for our infra folks. Thanks for the fast turnaround — we’ll review sandbox access and the SOC2 checklist when you send them.

—
Kayla

title: Staging-to-Prod sequence and enablement - RFI
preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -07:00
Subject: Staging-to-Prod sequence and enablement - RFI

Team —

We need to lock down the sequence and ownership for the upcoming staging->prod cutover for runtime-v2. Key ask: confirm the target production cutover window, who owns the playbook steps, and whether any known exceptions (3rd-party or config flags) need escalation.

Proposed constraints:
- Cutover weekend: Sat 2026-08-22 02:00–08:00 UTC (primary window) with a fallback on Sun 2026-08-23 same window
- Canary wave plan attached (rollout-waves.csv) — suggested waves: 5%, 25%, 50%, 100% over 12 hours
- Staging freeze: 2026-08-19 00:00 UTC (no schema/model changes after this)

Questions for each of you:
- Monica: can infra guarantee no rolling restarts beyond the planned GPU pool drain during waves? Any known dependency blockers?
- Ethan: doc & enablement lead — can you own the playbook and training slots? I've attached a draft playbook (pdf) and a doc-owners tracker xlsx.
- Grace: CS — do we need customer blackout dates for onboarding waves and do you have a prioritized recontact list?

Please reply with ownership + blockers by EOD Wednesday 2026-08-12 so I can surface to PM and Eng leaders.

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Program Manager, Launch Operations
Redwood Inference
(attachment: rollout-waves.csv, staging-cutover-playbook-v0.9.pdf, doc-owners-tracker.xlsx)


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 12:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Staging-to-Prod sequence and enablement - RFI

Jordan,

Thanks — quick status from infra/ops side:

- We can support the primary weekend window (2026-08-22 02:00–08:00 UTC). We'll need a 30-min pre-cutover health check and a 1-hour recovery reserve after each wave.
- Known constraint: the embeddings cache service (3rd-party cache) has a TTL edge-case that can cause ~30s cold-start latency for a small percentage of requests after a model swap. Ops is investigating a mitigation but we should include an exception step in the playbook that toggles a short-lived fallback routing policy.
- No planned rolling restarts aside from the GPU node drain we schedule for kernel swaps, but that increases tail latency risk for long-running requests. Recommend limiting in-flight long-context requests during waves and enabling shorter request timeouts.

Blockers / asks for other teams:
- We need a doc entry that lists request-timeout recommended values per route (Ethan)
- If CS plans to contact customers ahead of their wave, provide a list so ops can prioritize escalation channels

I'll take the infra checklist in the playbook and add an "exceptions" appendix for the embeddings cache and KMS key rotation.

-Monica
Monica Patel
Head of Release Engineering, Redwood Inference
(attachment referenced: staging-cutover-playbook-v0.9.pdf - will add appendix)


From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Aug 2026 08:37:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Staging-to-Prod sequence and enablement - RFI

Folks —

I can own the playbook doc and the enablement runbook. Quick notes:

title: Intro: GitHub app/webhook auth questions + demo scheduling
preview:
From: Alex Johnson <alex.j@openforge.dev>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: GitHub app/webhook auth questions + demo?

Hi Ethan,

I just signed up for Redwood via the GitHub onboarding link (we're an OSS maintainer team at OpenForge). I played with the self-serve flow and had a couple of quick questions before we pull in the rest of the engineering team: 

- Does the GitHub integration use a GitHub App or an OAuth app under the hood? We need to understand install scopes for org-wide installs.
- How are webhooks authenticated? We prefer secret-based HMACs or a signed header so our receivers can validate payloads.
- If we want the model to access repo files, what permissions are strictly required? We're sensitive about read/write scopes.

Also, we'd like to see Redwood in action. Can we schedule a short demo (30 min) focused on integration, webhook flow, and recommended scopes for OSS projects? We're mostly PST, but some folks are EST. Calendly link would be easiest.

Thanks,
Alex
Alex Johnson
Principal Engineer, OpenForge
alex.j@openforge.dev
http://openforge.dev

[Note: I attached a brief checklist of our integration questions as a PDF]

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
To: Alex Johnson <alex.j@openforge.dev>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 16:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: GitHub app/webhook auth questions + demo?

Hi Alex — great to meet you and thanks for signing up. Quick answers up front, and happy to demo any of this live: 

- Install model: Redwood supports both GitHub App-based installs (recommended for org-level installs) and OAuth-based token flows for single-user integrations. For org installs we typically recommend a GitHub App to avoid org token sharing.
- Webhook auth: we send HMAC-SHA256 signatures (X-Redwood-Signature) and also support signed payload headers for extra validation.
- Repo access: read-only repo access is sufficient for most use cases (indexing code, generating embeddings). Write scopes are only needed for actions like automated PR comments or status updates; we recommend granting minimal scopes and using fine-grained tokens where possible.

Soojin (SDR) — can you share a couple of 30-min slots this week that cover a short walkthrough + integration Q&A? Prefer late PST so Alex's team can join. I attached a short demo agenda and an integration checklist I promised.

Alex — here are two options if you'd like to pick immediately: 
- Tue 2026-08-04 11:30 PDT / 14:30 EDT (30m)
- Wed 2026-08-05 09:00 PDT / 12:00 EDT (30m)

Or use our calendar: https://calendly.com/redwood/demo-30m (we'll tailor the agenda).

Thanks,
Ethan
Ethan Park
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0192

Attachments: redwood-demo-agenda.odt (agenda), openforge-integration-checklist.pdf (your notes)

> - Webhook auth: we send HMAC-SHA256 signatures (X-Redwood-Signature) and also support signed payload headers for extra validation.
> - Repo access: read-only repo access is sufficient for most use cases (indexing code, generating embeddings). Write scopes are only needed for actions like automated PR comments or status updates; we recommend granting minimal scopes and using fine-grained tokens where possible.

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>

Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 16:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: GitHub app/webhook auth questions + demo?

Hi Alex — great to meet you and thanks for signing up. Quick answers up front, and happy to demo any of this live: 

- Install model: Redwood supports both GitHub App-based installs (recommended for org-level installs) and OAuth-based token flows for single-user integrations. For org installs we typically recommend a GitHub App to avoid org token sharing.
- Webhook auth: we send HMAC-SHA256 signatures (X-Redwood-Signature) and also support signed payload headers for extra validation.
- Repo access: read-only repo access is sufficient for most use cases (indexing code, generating embeddings). Write scopes are only needed for actions like automated PR comments or status updates; we recommend granting minimal scopes and using fine-grained tokens where possible.

Soojin (SDR) — can you share a couple of 30-min slots this week that cover a short walkthrough + integration Q&A? Prefer late PST so Alex's team can join. I attached a short demo agenda and an integration checklist I promised.

title: Clarifying billing model for rerank/classifier scoring
preview:
From: Jenna Morales <jenna.morales@perchanalytics.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 5 Jul 2026 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Clarifying billing for classifier/rerank endpoints

Hi Priya,

Quick question on pricing: we're evaluating Redwood for a rerank + classifier pipeline and want to understand how scoring is billed on the hosted API. A few specifics:

- If we send a single rerank request with 50 candidate items, is that billed as one API call or 50 scores?
- Do you have a separate unit for classifier scoring vs. reranker scoring?
- We're planning a batch job that will score ~1,000,000 items/day (one pass per item). How should we model costs for that workload?

Also: any standard rate card you can share (baseline hosted pricing + volume tiers) and whether there are credits available for a short POC would be helpful.

Thanks,
Jenna Morales
Head of Data Products
Perch Analytics
jenna.morales@perchanalytics.com

---

Attachment: none


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Jenna Morales <jenna.morales@perchanalytics.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Jul 2026 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarifying billing for classifier/rerank endpoints

Hi Jenna — thanks for the clear questions. Short answers up front, then a couple of follow-ups so I can give a concrete estimate.

Summary (baseline hosted API):
- Rerank/classifier scoring is metered primarily on a per-score basis for candidate-heavy requests. Practically, that means a single rerank call with 50 candidates counts as 50 scoring units.
- There is also a per-request component for routing/overhead (small, single-digit fractional cents); for most customers the per-score unit dominates costs for candidate-heavy workloads.
- Classifier-only calls (single label) are billed as a single scoring unit per item unless you request multiple label predictions.

Baseline prices (hosted API, public list):
- Per-score (base LLM profile): $0.0009 per score
- Per-score (high-accuracy profile / larger model): $0.0035 per score
- Per-request overhead: $0.0001 per call

For your batch of ~1,000,000 items/day that would be: 1,000,000 * per-score rate * days. Example (base profile): 1,000,000 * $0.0009 = $900/day (~$27k/month).

Questions so we can tighten the estimate:
1) For the rerank flow, how many candidates per item do you typically score (average and 95th percentile)? You mentioned 50 in your note — is that average or peak?
2) Are you doing one-off batch scoring (one pass per item per day) or frequent incremental scoring?
3) Any need for guaranteed latency/SLA or is a cost-optimized background batch approach OK?

I'm attaching the initial rate card (spreadsheet) so you can see tiered discounts and reserved options. If you can share expected monthly scoring volume (or a range), I can run committed-tiers math and a reserved capacity option that often lowers unit price substantially.

Best,
Priya Natarajan
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
priya.natarajan@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0127

Attachments: per-ch-initial-rate-card-v1.xlsx (spreadsheet with list tiers)

From: Jenna Morales <jenna.morales@perchanalytics.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 7 Jul 2026 10:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarifying billing for classifier/rerank endpoints

Thanks, Priya — helpful. A few clarifications:

- 50 is our average candidates per query; the 95th percentile is ~120.
- The 1,000,000 items/day is a nightly batch that re-scores a large catalog once per day. We may also score smaller real-time rerank queries during the day against the top 50 candidates from a pre-filter.
- Latency for batch is not critical; for real-time queries we prefer <200ms p95 but that's not the bulk of traffic.

A couple of asks:
1) For the batch use-case, does Redwood offer a bulk/ingest credit model (e.g., one-time discounted credit for high-volume uploads) or should we look at committed monthly scoring tiers?
2) Does prefix/KV caching reduce billed scores (if you cache embeddings or partial outputs)?
3) Can you provide a starter committed tier for roughly 30M scores/month with a target unit price in the $0.0005–$0.0007 range? If not, what does Reserved/Committed get us vs on-demand?

If you can put something in writing (even a draft proposal) we'll route to procurement. Also cc'ing our procurement alias for visibility.

Thanks,
Jenna
Procurement cc: procure@perchanalytics.com

---

Quoted earlier message:
> Baseline prices (hosted API, public list):
> - Per-score (base LLM profile): $0.0009 per score
> - Per-score (high-accuracy profile / larger model): $0.0035 per score
>


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Jenna Morales <jenna.morales@perchanalytics.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Jul 2026 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarifying billing for classifier/rerank endpoints

Thanks for the details, Jenna — looping in Dev (engineer) and Ethan (finance) to propose concrete options. Draft math and options below; attaching a short proposal PDF.

Quick answers:
- Caching: KV/prefix caching reduces compute and effective cost for real-time flows, but for standalone batch scoring (full re-score of each item) caching doesn't reduce per-score billing since each item is being evaluated. For pre-filter->top-K flows, caching can reduce the number of candidates you actually score live and thus reduce billed scores.

Options (30M scores/month target):
A) Consumption (on-demand):
   - Base per-score: $0.0009
   - Example cost: 30,000,000 * $0.0009 = $27,000/month

B) Committed 12-month tier: 15% discount on per-score base for a 12-month prepay/commit (minimum commit 20M scores/month average).
   - Effective unit: $0.000765 per score -> 30M * $0.000765 = $22,950/month

C) Reserved capacity (Dedicated pools):
   - Reserved pool priced by GPU-hour and includes a predictable throughput SLA. For very heavy workloads with steady qps this often yields deeper discounts on a per-score-equivalent basis; let's evaluate if you want a back-of-envelope.

D) One-time onboarding credit: we can provide 10k free scored items for POC and an additional $2,000 credit toward first-quarter billing for new enterprise commitments.

Attached: perch-proposal-draft.pdf (contains tier tables and a worked example for your 30M/month target).

If the committed tier looks promising, Ethan can draft a formal quote for procurement and propose language for volume tiers and overage handling.

Next step suggestions:
- If you want sticker pricing we can send a formal quote (Ethan) and a DocuSign for a 12-month commitment.
- If you'd like technical input on reducing average candidates via pre-filtering, Dev can hop on a 30-minute call to review options.

Best,
Priya

Attachments: perch-proposal-draft.pdf


From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
To: Jenna Morales <jenna.morales@perchanalytics.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Jul 2026 09:40:00 -0700
Subject: Final rate card and next steps - Perch Analytics (D-3491)

Hi Jenna / team — following up with a formal summary and the attached final rate card. Key points: 

- Public (on-demand) per-score: $0.0009
- Committed 12-month (20M min/month): $0.000765 per score (15% off list)
- Reserved Dedicated pool: custom pricing — typically 20–35% cost improvement for steady-state scoring workloads, we can model this if you share peak qps and batch windows.

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Jenna Morales <jenna.morales@perchanalytics.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Jul 2026 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarifying billing for classifier/rerank endpoints

Hi Jenna — thanks for the clear questions. Short answers up front, then a couple of follow-ups so I can give a concrete estimate.

Summary (baseline hosted API):
- Rerank/classifier scoring is metered primarily on a per-score basis for candidate-heavy requests. Practically, that means a single rerank call with 50 candidates counts as 50 scoring units.
- There is also a per-request component for routing/overhead (small, single-digit fractional cents); for most customers the per-score unit dominates costs for candidate-heavy workloads.
- Classifier-only calls (single label) are billed as a single scoring unit per item unless you request multiple label predictions.

Baseline prices (hosted API, public list):
- Per-score (base LLM profile): $0.0009 per score
- Per-score (high-accuracy profile / larger model): $0.0035 per score
- Per-request overhead: $0.0001 per call

For your batch of ~1,000,000 items/day that would be: 1,000,000 * per-score rate * days. Example (base profile): 1,000,000 * $0.0009 = $900/day (~$27k/month).

Questions so we can tighten the estimate:
1) For the rerank flow, how many candidates per item do you typically score (average and 95th percentile)? You mentioned 50 in your note — is that average or peak?
2) Are you doing one-off batch scoring (one pass per item per day) or frequent incremental scoring?
3) Any need for guaranteed latency/SLA or is a cost-optimized background batch approach OK?

I'm attaching the initial rate card (spreadsheet) so you can see tiered discounts and reserved options. If you can share expected monthly scoring volume (or a range), I can run committed-tiers math and a reserved capacity option that often lowers unit price substantially.

C) Reserved capacity (Dedicated pools):

D) One-time onboarding credit: we can provide 10k free scored items for POC and an additional $2,000 credit toward first-quarter billing for new enterprise commitments.

Date: Wed, 08 Jul 2026 16:12:00 -0700
Subject: GreenGrid: observed throughput drop + ask for temporary routing exception

Team,

GreenGrid flagged a recurring throughput drop during their evening batch windows (US West peak) starting yesterday at ~18:30 PDT. They reported 40-60% drop in tokens/sec with elevated tail latency for 30-45m. Attached is their summary (greengrid_spike_summary.pdf) and the early traces we pulled (redwood_diagnostics_trace.json).

Immediate asks:
- Can SRE/Eng evaluate whether we can enable a temporary routing exception to route GreenGrid to a higher-throughput pool (dedicated capacity) for the next 72 hours to stabilize customer SLAs?
- If routing exception is feasible, what exact config changes and risks should we call out to the customer?
- Product: is there a quick config/feature toggle we can expose (burst capacity flag) while we investigate root cause and a longer-term fix?

Tom (cc'd), I want to keep you looped — we are triaging and will propose a short notice window if we need to move you to a different pool.

Next steps I propose: SRE/Eng triage within 60m, internal decision and customer-ready note within 3 hours.

Thanks,
Priya Natarajan
Account Exec, Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

Attachments: greengrid_spike_summary.pdf (pdf summary), redwood_diagnostics_trace.json (text), spike_kpis.csv (csv)


From: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Hannah Schmitt <hannah@redwood.com>, Tom Brewer <tom.brewer@greengrid.com>, ops@greengrid.com
Date: Wed, 08 Jul 2026 16:34:00 -0700
Subject: Re: GreenGrid: observed throughput drop + ask for temporary routing exception

Priya — thanks for the callout and the artifacts. Quick summary from initial triage (30m):
- We see elevated queueing on the request ingress and increased KV-cache misses for mid-length sequences (64-256).
- The spike aligns with a change in the customer's traffic pattern (more concurrent short-chat sessions) and coincides with a degradation in slot utilization on the multi-tenant pool.
- No evidence yet of kernel-level faulting or OOM; node CPU/GPU metrics look within expected ranges but scheduling latency increased.

Temporary routing exception: feasible as a short-lived mitigation. Options:
1) Route their traffic to a dedicated burst pool (if capacity available) — fastest, requires capacity reservation + config change at the routing layer.
2) Apply per-tenant concurrency cap and adaptive batching to reduce queueing — less disruptive but may reduce throughput for some request shapes.

Risk notes: 1) Moving to a higher-throughput pool may require changing model slice and has a 5-10 min cutover; 2) If we keep them multi-tenant, other tenants may see small jitter.

I'll open a JIRA and start a provisional plan. Dev, can your team validate whether we can flip routing for GG-ENTERPRISE-42 without a code deploy? If yes, estimate time to cut and rollback.

Ethan
SRE Lead, Redwood Inference

----

- We'll need approvals for capacity reservation (finance/ops) if the burst uses billed reserved slots. Estimated safe window: 72 hours. Cutover plan: 5–10 min for traffic drain + warmup. Rollback: instant re-route back to multi-tenant.
- Two caveats: 1) if their model variant differs and isn't present on the dedicated pool we need a hot-pull (5–15 min image warm). 2) We should throttle concurrent startup to avoid cold-cache storm.

preview:
From: Lisa Raymond <lisa@coreapi.io>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Miles <jordan@coreapi.io>
Date: Thu, 02 Jul 2026 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Sustained 5xx/429 spike — urgent: our customers are impacted

Subject: Unexpected June invoice — token double-count?

Hi Hannah,

We received our June invoice this morning (attached) and are seeing a ~2x spike in token counts compared to our dashboard and expected usage pattern. Can you confirm whether this is a billing issue or if something on our side caused duplicate requests? Invoice total: $12,348.02 (invoice_NOVUS_202606.pdf attached).

We use the Redwood hosted API (prod key tied to HV-3245). We noticed the spike around 2026-06-14 00:00 UTC and again on 2026-06-21; our app logs show retries but not at the scale of the billed tokens. We need to understand quickly — can your team review for duplicate counting or replay?

Thanks for the quick look — Sara
Sara Kim
Head of Engineering, Novus Health

From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah@redwood.ai>
To: Sara Kim <sara.kim@novushealth.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 02 Jul 2026 16:40:00 -0700
Subject: Resolution: token recount, credit issued, and postmortem link (Novus Health)

Hi Sara — following up with final findings and the customer-facing remediation plan. Thanks for your patience.

Summary (what happened)
- Root cause: We had a transient router replay bug during an autoscaling event on 2026-06-14 that caused certain in-flight requests to be replayed to the inference runtime. The replay path did not deduplicate at the token-accounting layer for a short window, which resulted in duplicate token counts for affected requests.
- Affected window: 2026-06-14 00:00 UTC — 2026-06-14 03:30 UTC (primary), with a smaller replay burst on 2026-06-21 11:45 UTC — 2026-06-21 12:10 UTC.
- Scope: 11% of Novus' billed tokens for June were double-counted; total overbill ≈ $1,362.40.

Immediate remediation we applied
- We issued a customer credit of $1,362.40 against your July billing cycle. Credit notice attached (credit_notice_NOVUS_2026-07-02.pdf).
- Engineering implemented a quick server-side fix in the routing layer to dedupe replayed requests at the accounting boundary. The change was rolled to prod on 2026-06-30 and validated.
- We created a short-term monitoring alert and throttle for autoscaling windows to prevent a recurrence while we complete a full postmortem.

Deliverables and next steps
- Postmortem: public-facing postmortem document is linked here for review: https://drive.redwood.ai/postmortems/2026-06-token-recount (internal full doc attached as postmortem_token_recount_2026-06.docx).
- Compliance: We retained request logs and counters and are ready to share filtered CSVs for your auditors if needed.
- Confirmation: please reply to confirm the credit applied and whether you need the audit extracts or a different remediation timeline.

We're sorry for the disruption to your billing and are treating this as a priority reliability gap. Let me know if you'd like a call to walk through the postmortem — Avery and Marcus are available early next week.

Best,
Hannah Schmitt
Account Lead, Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
hannah@redwood.ai

---
attachments: postmortem_token_recount_2026-06.docx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document), credit_notice_NOVUS_2026-07-02.pdf (application/pdf)

- redwood-rw-7b: precision 0.68, hallucination 0.09 — fast TTFT but higher hallucination on long-context generative answers
- redwood-rw-13b: precision 0.77, hallucination 0.05 — better quality; TTFT ~1.2x of 7b at steady 10c
- redwood-rw-13b-4bit (quant): precision 0.74, hallucination 0.06 — quality close to 13b, median TTFT improved vs 13b by ~20%, sustained throughput comparable to 13b under 10c

Attached is a combined perf+quality matrix and a recommended pilot: redwood-rw-13b-4bit for Atlas pilot (best balance of latency and quality, reduced cost). See MAP_Final_0629.pdf for suggested Dedicated sizing and SLA language.

title: SDK routing fallback: customer asks for controlled model fallback & exception playbook
preview:
From: Alex Lopez <alex@opticai.io>
To: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 24 Jun 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Controlled fallback behavior for SDK — urgent ask

Hi Stephanie —

We had a call with the product team this morning and one item bubbled up as blocking for our pilot: when the primary model is at capacity or we intentionally route to a lower-cost variant, we need the SDK to support a per-request routing hint (prefer_preferred_model or prefer_cost_saving) and a deterministic fallback policy when a variant is unavailable. Right now the behavior feels opaque — requests occasionally hit a cheaper quantized model and we lose a small but important slice of accuracy.

Can Redwood provide a short playbook: what headers/SDK flags we can set, how to pin requests to a model family, and an exception path we can use when we detect output regressions? We need this for our internal compliance review and to finish the integration checklist.

Appreciate a quick turnaround — pilot goes live next week.

Thanks,
Alex
Head of Product, OpticAI

Attachments: opticai_routing_case.json (small sample traces)


From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 24 Jun 2026 10:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Controlled fallback behavior for SDK — urgent ask

Folks — forwarding OpticAI's ask (below). They want: per-request routing hint + deterministic fallback + an exception path (manual override or audit). Pilot is next week so they'd like a concise playbook and any quick SDK changes we can ship as a patch or config.

Questions I want us to resolve before I reply to Alex:
- Is there already a request header or SDK flag we can recommend today for \"prefer_cost_saving\"/\"prefer_accuracy\"?
- If a model is down/unavailable, what's the guaranteed fallback chain? Is it deterministic by model family & quantization?
- Can we offer a short-term patch (SDK config) to let a client pin to a model version (and fail fast if not available) vs. current graceful fallback?

Marissa — I'll need you on the reply to customer. Dev/Karthik/Ethan, can you triage feasibility and risk?

-Stephanie
Director, Solutions Engineering
Redwood Inference


From: Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>
To: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 24 Jun 2026 11:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Controlled fallback behavior for SDK — urgent ask

Steph — quick technical status:
- Today: SDK supports model_name and model_version fields. The runtime may reroute to a compatible variant (same family) if the exact version is unavailable — that behavior is currently \"best-effort\" and influenced by routing policies (cost vs latency SLO). There is no per-request honor flag to force cost vs accuracy preference beyond selecting a model family.
- Deterministic fallback: we have a fallback priority list per deployment (e.g., familyA:v2 -> familyA:v1 -> quantized familyA:int8), but it can change during autoscaling and capacity events. So it's not strictly deterministic for a request when load-based dynamic routing happens.
- Quick patch idea: we can add a boolean SDK flag prefer_exact_version=true that instructs the control plane to fail fast if the exact model_version isn't available (no fallback). That would be a low-risk API/SDK change and reachable in ~3 business days for an SDK release (plus runtime gating to honor the header).
- Longer run: add prefer_cost_saving/prefer_accuracy triage enum that maps to routing policies. That needs product buy-in and a small control-plane UI change.

Attached a short internal doc: routing_playbook_v1.pdf (notes on current runtime behavior and quick mitigations).

Dev
Senior Software Engineer, Inference Runtime


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 24 Jun 2026 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Controlled fallback behavior for SDK — urgent ask

Thanks Dev — summary and product decision:
- Short-term: approve prefer_exact_version flag. Behavior: when set, the control plane will return a 409 (ModelUnavailable) if the exact model_version is not routable; SDK should surface the error and optionally return the raw body. This avoids silent fallback regressions. Dev, target ETA: 72 hours for SDK + control-plane gating with a feature flag.
- Medium-term: add routing_preference enum (cost|accuracy|latency) that ties into the autoscaler and routing policies. We'll scope as a Q3 work item and create a lightweight UI toggle for Dedicated customers.
- Exception playbook: for customers who need both cost savings and auditability, we can recommend attaching request-level telemetry and using prefer_exact_version + post-request regression detection (we'll provide a sample check).

Marissa — you should share the approved short-term approach with OpticAI and offer the experimental flag under a feature flag with a note about 409 behavior. Ethan, help create an internal rollout checklist for the control-plane change and coordinate with infra.

-Karthik
Director, Product Management


From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Alex Lopez <alex@opticai.io>
Cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 25 Jun 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Controlled fallback behavior for SDK — urgent ask

Alex — thanks for the clear ask and the sample traces. Quick plan and next steps: 

1) Short-term (72 hours): we will provide an SDK flag prefer_exact_version=true and a control-plane feature flag that makes the runtime fail fast (ModelUnavailable 409) rather than silently routing to a different variant. This prevents accidental accuracy regressions. We expect to deliver a patched SDK build and a short playbook (how to interpret 409 + recommended retries) within 72 hours.

2) Medium-term (Q3): we will add a routing_preference enum (cost|accuracy|latency) that maps to routing policies; this will let you express intent without pinning. We will invite OpticAI to an early access program.

3) Exception path / audit: use prefer_exact_version for production-critical calls; additionally, include request_id in your payload so you can correlate responses. We'll attach a short regression-detection snippet you can drop into your post-processing pipeline.

Would you be available for a 30-minute call tomorrow at 10am PT to walk the playbook and run the sample traces live? If that works we'll send a calendar invite and a temporary feature-flag key.

Best,
Marissa Cole
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: routing_playbook_v1.pdf


From: Alex Lopez <alex@opticai.io>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 25 Jun 2026 11:29:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Controlled fallback behavior for SDK — urgent ask

Marissa — this is great news, thank you. 10am PT tomorrow works; please send calendar invite. We'll enable prefer_exact_version for our critical endpoints and run parallel comparison for 24 hours.

One operational ask: can Redwood provide a short snippet that turns a 409 into a non-retryable error in the SDK (i.e., so our retry middleware doesn't mask it)? Also, can you confirm whether the prefer_exact_version flag is supported for both Hosted API and Dedicated?

Thanks — Alex


Note: attachments referenced above: routing_playbook_v1.pdf, opticai_routing_case.json

title: Re: Scheduling demo — self‑serve signup follow up / quick qualification

To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>

Subject: Demo request — evaluating Redwood for embeddings/search

Hi Ethan,

I signed up for the self-serve trial this morning and am evaluating Redwood for our embeddings + semantic search use case.

Quick context: we run a customer support search layer over product docs and past tickets. Current volume is roughly 400k queries/month but expected to grow to ~1M within 6–9 months. Latency at tail and token cost are pain points for us.

Would love a short demo to see how Redwood handles KV cache, batching, and whether Dedicated/reserved options make sense for our traffic profile. We’re aiming to pilot in Q3, ideally starting a 6–8 week PoC in July.

Alex

alex.j@brightdata.io

- Primary goal: are you focused on improving end-user search latency, lowering inference cost, or reducing variability for SLOs?
- Typical sequence length for queries and doc chunks (tokens)? e.g., short queries (~10–30 tokens) searching against 1–2k tokens doc chunks, or longer contexts?
- Which models/embedding stacks are you evaluating right now (open-source family, managed models, hybrid)?
- Who do you expect to attend the demo from your side (technical PM, infra lead, security)? I ask because we can include a short slide on Dedicated/Private if security is a concern.

Proposed slots (ET):
- Wed 06/23 09:30–10:00
- Wed 06/23 11:00–11:30
- Thu 06/24 14:30–15:00

If none work, send over a preferred link (Calendly/meeting invite) and I’ll coordinate.

Cheers,
Ethan Park
Senior SDR, Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwood.ai

title: Re: RFI - Dedicated Inference Capacity: GPU types, throughput & reservation plan
preview:
From: Mikaela Johnson <mikaela.j@atlashealth.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@atlashealth.com
Date: 2026-06-01T09:15:00Z
Subject: RFI: Dedicated Inference Capacity — throughput & GPU sizing

Hi Neha,

Thanks for taking this RFI. We're starting the procurement cycle for dedicated inference capacity to support our care-coordination assistant. Attached is a short questionnaire but key items for us are: expected throughput (requests/sec and tokens/sec), supported GPU types (H100/A100/L4), tail latency at p99 under 128-token responses, and options for reserved commitments vs burst.

Can you confirm Redwood's recommended GPU mixes for a production 500 QPS conversational workload (avg 50 tokens/response), and what headroom you recommend for autoscaling/burst? Also please send any assumptions you use for batching and KV cache benefits.

Timeline: procurement board on 2026-06-22 — we'd like your answers by 2026-06-15.

Thanks,
Mikaela
Mikaela Johnson
Senior Procurement Manager
Atlas Health

Attachment: atlas_rfi_initial_questions.pdf (PDF)


From: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-02T10:04:00Z
Subject: Fwd: RFI: Dedicated Inference Capacity — throughput & GPU sizing (Atlas Health)

Team — forwarding Atlas' RFI. They need a detailed response by 2026-06-15 to make the procurement board. Can we split this:
- Karthik: drive recommended GPU mixes + pricing sensitivity for reserved vs burst (include H100/A100/L4 tradeoffs)
- Ethan: provide expected throughput numbers and p99 latency tables for 50/128/512 token responses (include batching assumptions)
- Laura: include notes on reservation planning, SLA options, and how Dedicated vs Private differs for compliance.

I'll assemble and send to Mikaela. Please use the atlas-usage-patterns-2026-06.csv they attached and call out any gaps.

Neha
Neha Kapoor
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
neha@redwood.com



From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-10T15:32:00Z
Subject: Re: Fwd: RFI: Dedicated Inference Capacity — Atlas Health (draft answers)

Neha / team — draft below. I attached a one-page PDF that summarizes the numbers; putting the rationale inline here for quick review.

Executive summary (draft): For a steady-state 500 QPS / 50 tokens per response conversational workload (≈25k tokens/sec), we recommend a mixed pool: baseline of H100 (1:2) + A100 (1:3) with L4 instances for low-cost inference of short responses or embeddings. That mix gives ~30% headroom for burst and allows lower-cost serving for mixed latency SLAs.

Assumptions:
- Model: MPT-7B-Chat-like single-sharded runtime or equivalent open 70B quantized where H100 provides ~2.5x throughput vs A100 for long sequences.
- Batching: effective batching factor of 8 on average; continuous batching with 10-20ms wait window.
- KV cache hit rate: 35% for our conversational traffic profile (saves ~25% of tokens executed on GPU).
- p99 target: 250–350ms on H100 for 128-token responses (w/ batching and cache), ~400–550ms on A100. L4 is sub-200ms for short responses but not suitable for long-context heavy workloads.

Reservation planning:
- Recommend initial 3-month commit at 60% of projected peak, with monthly review and an option to scale to 80% commit at 6 months.
- Burst pool: 20–40% unreserved capacity across regions for unpredictability.

I'll wait for Ethan's latency tables; Laura can add the SLA/reservation language. If this looks good I'll include the numbers in the final PDF.

Attachment: Redwood_Dedicated_Capacity_Draft_20260610.pdf (PDF)

Thanks,
Karthik
Account Executive — Redwood Inference


From: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
To: Mikaela Johnson <mikaela.j@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>, procurement@atlashealth.com
Date: 2026-06-15T11:22:00Z
Subject: Re: RFI - Dedicated Inference Capacity: GPU types, throughput & reservation plan

Mikaela — thanks for the clear timeline. Attached are our consolidated answers and a short appendix with per-GPU throughput & p99 latency tables derived from our benchmark harness using your sample traffic. Key highlights:

- Recommended mix for 500 QPS (avg 50 tokens): 8x H100 + 12x A100 + optional 6x L4 for low-cost short-response traffic. This set supports steady 500 QPS with ~30% burst headroom in most regions.
- Throughput assumptions: ~25k tokens/sec baseline; batching factor 7–9; KV cache hit rate assumed 30–40% (we include sensitivity rows at 20%/50%).
- Latency: p99 for 128-token responses on H100 = 320ms (incl batching window); A100 p99 = 480ms; L4 p99 (<=64 tokens) = 180ms. See attached tables.
- Reservation plan: recommend \"staged commit\" — start with a 3-month 60% commit, with calendar-aligned upsell to 80% commit at 6 months if traffic growth matches. We offer SLA credits for failures and an emergency burst pool for 30% above reserved capacity for 24-hour windows.
- Compatibility: all instances support model pinning and automatic fallback to smaller quantized variants when long-tail latency events occur.

Attachments: Redwood_Dedicated_Capacity_Answers_20260615.pdf, atlas-usage-patterns-2026-06.csv

Next steps: please confirm if you'd like a follow-up call to walk through the p99 table and cost sensitivity (we can share estimated monthly commit pricing for the three commit tiers). Happy to join your procurement board on 2026-06-22 if helpful.

Best,
Neha Kapoor
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
neha@redwood.com
Office: +1 (415) 555-0198



From: Mikaela Johnson <mikaela.j@atlashealth.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@atlashealth.com
Date: 2026-06-16T14:05:00Z
Subject: Re: RFI - Dedicated Inference Capacity: GPU types, throughput & reservation plan

Neha — thank you, this is very helpful and arrived right before our board packet deadline. Quick clarification: do you have region-specific availability for H100-based pools in us-west vs eu-west? Our compliance team prefers a us-west primary with eu for DR. Also, can you provide a short row showing incremental cost delta between 60% commit and 80% commit per month (ballpark numbers are fine for the board)?

If you can get those two bullets by EOD Monday (2026-06-20) we'll include them in the packet. Otherwise we may request a live 20-minute slot.

Appreciate the fast turnaround.
Mikaela
Senior Procurement Manager
Atlas Health

title: Weekly commit coordination: stages + evidence upload
preview:
From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
To: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: Weekly commit coordination: stages + evidence upload

Team —

Quick reminder ahead of today’s submit window. A few asks for the Acme lane and a couple other late-stage deals so Finance can reconcile forecast today:

title: Classifier & scoring economics — unit billing + bundle options
preview:
From: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@harrisanalytics.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-05-18T10:12:00-07:00
Subject: Quick question on rerank/classifier billing units

Hi Rachel,

Thanks for the demo last week — the latency numbers looked promising. Procurement asked me to get a clearer baseline on how Redwood bills \"scoring\" for rerank/classifier endpoints for hosted API usage. A few concrete questions: 

- Is reranking billed per input query (request) or per scored item?
- Do classifiers that return confidence scores count tokens + scoring units?
- Are there separate unit costs for model family (e.g., small vs large) or is it a single per-scoring unit price?

We'd like to model monthly costs for a pilot at ~3M queries/month with average 128 candidate items per query (batch scored concurrently). If you have a short rate card or example math we can give procurement, that would be super helpful.

Also attaching a sample payload so you can see how our requests look.

Thanks,
Oliver
Senior Data Scientist
Harris Analytics

Attached: sample_payload.json (JSON)

From: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
To: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@harrisanalytics.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-05-19T09:04:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Quick question on rerank/classifier billing units

Hi Oliver — great to hear from you and glad the demo was useful. High-level answers up front, then a simple worked example.

Short answers:
- Rerank: billed per scored item by default (\"per-score\") for the rerank endpoint. There is also a per-request component for baseline processing on the request/response overhead.
- Classifier w/ confidence: you pay token usage (generation) plus a flat per-classification unit if you call the dedicated classifier endpoint.
- Model family: we publish tiered unit prices by model family (small/med/large) because larger models have higher token and compute costs.

Worked example (pilot you mentioned):
- 3,000,000 queries/mo x 128 items = 384,000,000 scored items/mo.
- If per-score unit = $0.0000018 (example small-model rate) => $691.20/mo in scoring units.
- Per-request baseline (metadata + request handling) 3M x $0.00005 = $150.00/mo.
- Token generation for classifier outputs would be additional and depends on avg tokens per response.

Notes/assumptions:
- The per-score rate varies by model/quantization profile and whether you enable prefix caching/continuous batching (we model those as lowered effective price in Optimize).
- We have two common packaging approaches: 
  1) Usage-only (pay-as-you-go) — good for pilot.
  2) Committed bundle (monthly commit) — gives a discount on per-score units and priority capacity.

I'll attach a brief rate card PDF (v2) and include a simple spreadsheet view if useful. If you want, we can also produce per-variant math for your exact candidate size and seq lengths.

Would you like me to put together a short pilot quote with a 10–20% committed discount? If procurement prefers a flat monthly line for budgeting we can model that too.

Best,
Rachel Kim
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Date: 2026-06-07T15:04:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Classifier & scoring economics — unit billing + bundle options

Hi Oliver and procurement team — thanks for your patience. Per our internal review I've attached a short rate card (rate_card_v2.pdf) and two packaging options below.

Summary (pilot pricing):
- Usage-only: keep baseline rates by model family (see attachment). Example medium-family per-score = $0.0000026 pre-discount. For your 3M x 128 scenario that maps to $1,267.20/mo pre-token/additional overhead.
- Committed pilot: 3-month commit at 12% discount => medium-family per-score = $0.000002288 => estimated $1,116.26/mo (same assumptions).

Two packaging options we recommend:
1) Short pilot (usage-only + Optimize consult): 1 month free Optimize tuning, no commit. This is fastest to start and good if you want to test two-stage filtering.
2) 3-month committed pilot: 12% discount, 1 Optimize tuning session, 5 days of priority onboarding. If you later convert to Dedicated we can roll credits forward.

Answers to the other questions:
- Multi-stage pipelines: each endpoint call is billed independently. If you call a cheap filter (cheap-model classifier) then a separate rerank call, both calls incur their respective units. If you implement the filter client-side and only send final candidates, you're only billed for the rerank call.
- Batching/caching: our Optimize team can often reduce effective per-score cost by ~20–40% depending on cache-hit rates and candidate distribution. We'll include recommended configs in the onboarding doc.

Next steps: let me know which packaging you prefer and I will send a formal quote (DocuSign link) and include the pilot SLA.

Best,
Rachel Kim
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Acme's procurement and accounts-payable are on the thread in case you need to loop them in.

title: Post-call: token flow & peak vs steady-state sizing — next steps
preview:
From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
To: Alex Thompson <alex.thompson@novacart.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 28 May 2026 15:10:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: token flow & peak vs steady-state sizing — next steps

Alex — thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and clear next steps so we can size properly for NovaCart's checkout personalization use case.

Recap (short):
- Primary workload: real-time personalization at checkout. Requests are 1 prompt + user/cart context (~400 tokens) and we return a structured choice + explanation (~120 tokens).
- Typical steady-state: 45 QPS during business hours (US PST).
- Peak burst: up to 420 QPS for 60–90s during promotional events.
- Latency target: 95th percentile <= 120 ms for streaming responses (user sees partial choices as tokens stream).
- Concurrency: expected 600 concurrent inflight requests during peak (based on client-side retries + websockets).

Attachments: nova_token_profile.csv (export of sampled request sizes), peak-burst-sample-logs.zip (anonymized synthetic burst trace).

Immediate asks: 
1) Can you confirm the 420 QPS burst and whether it aligns with your load generator measurements?
2) Are there any additional long-context prompts ( > 2k tokens) we should account for?
3) Please share any SLO exceptions you need beyond the 95th/120 ms (e.g., 99th, tail latency allowances).

Proposed next steps (we can run a quick sizing exercise once you confirm numbers):
- Internal: map peak QPS -> tokens/sec and recommend Dedicated pool config + streaming slab.
- Ops: run a 1-hour simulated promo burst with your generator and our tooling; collect kv-cache warmup numbers.
- Commercial: estimate committed capacity and sketch monthly cost at projected utilization.

I’ll loop Ethan and Marissa for engineering + commercial follow-up. If this matches your notes, reply with the confirmations and any additional traces.

Thanks,
--
Soojin Lee
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
soojin.lee@redwood.com
Redwood Inference



From: Alex Thompson <alex.thompson@novacart.com>
To: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Rao <priya.rao@novacart.com>
Date: Thu, 28 May 2026 15:37:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: token flow & peak vs steady-state sizing — next steps

Soojin — thanks for the clear summary. Quick answers:
1) 420 QPS is our internal estimate for the peak promo spike; our load generator validated 380–460 QPS in three dry runs. We prefer to plan for 460 to be safe.
2) No >2k token prompts for checkout personalization today; we do have occasional batch jobs for campaign synthesis (~8k tokens) but those are offline and schedulable.
3) We care about 99th percentile more for checkout flows — would like the 99th <= 200 ms if possible.

I’m attaching a short CSV export from our test harness with timestamped QPS and token sizes (we stripped user IDs). Happy to run another focused burst if you want a different trace pattern.

—Alex



From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 28 May 2026 16:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Post-call: token flow & peak vs steady-state sizing — NovaCart answers + inputs

Ethan/Marissa — forwarding NovaCart's confirmations. Key updates: they want us to plan for 460 QPS peak, 45 QPS steady, 99th <= 200 ms target, and no long streaming contexts beyond checkout (good). They also have offline 8k token batch jobs we can treat as scheduled workloads.

Please see attachments (nova_token_profile.csv, nova_test_export.csv in the thread). Requests:
- Engineering: draft a baseline Dedicated config (GPU type + count), expected tokens/sec numbers, and estimated cost for 70%/30% steady:burst utilization split. Prioritize streaming path and KV-cache warmup behavior — burst durations are short but high amplitude.
- EvOps: plan a 1-hour run next Wed (6/3) where we coordinate their generator; collect 95/99 latencies and token throughput numbers.
- Commercial: produce a commissionable pricing sketch for a three-month pilot with a ramp to persistent Dedicated if metrics look good.

Marissa — can you take point on commercial and schedule the run with their SREs? Ethan, happy to sync on perf assumptions tomorrow.

--
Soojin



From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
To: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 28 May 2026 17:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Post-call: token flow & peak vs steady-state sizing — NovaCart answers + inputs

Acknowledged. I’ll pull preliminary numbers this evening but initial sizing notes:
- Using our streaming runtime assumptions, 460 QPS * (avg 520 tokens/request) -> ~239k tokens/sec peak.
- On A100-ish Dedicated slab with continuous batching and prefix cache, rough starting point = 4x A100 GPUs (slotting in our "burst slab" profile) to meet 95/99 targets with modest headroom; if they can tolerate 250 ms 99th we can drop to 3x.
- KV-cache warmup: first 30–90s of a burst will see higher tail latency as caches populate; we should capture this in the run and include a recommended warmup policy (pre-warming during expected promo windows).

Action items for tomorrow: Ethan -> concrete GPU/slot recommendation + expected token/sec per GPU; Marissa -> confirm commercial pilot terms and coordinate Wed 6/3 run with NovaCart.

Thanks,
Ethan
Principal Performance Engineer, Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwood.com

From: Jane Doe <jane.doe@bluewavemed.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@bluewavemed.com
Date: Wed, 20 May 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: VPC pricing breakdown & support options

Ethan / Marissa —

Thanks for the demo yesterday. BlueWave is evaluating a VPC deployment for a pilot (healthcare data, regional US deployment) and procurement asked for a compact commercial package we can review with legal and infra. Specifically we need:

- A clear split between the perpetual license vs. platform fee (how the platform fee is invoiced / amortized for pilots).
- Support tiers with response times and on-call coverage options (SLA matrix).
- Any pilot discount you can offer for a 3–6 month trial, and whether usage commit changes pricing.
- A one-page SOW stub and the standard redwood commercial term sheet we can circulate.

I’ve attached a short SOW draft and the procurement team’s timeline: procurement will aim for an approval decision by the end of June, PO by mid-July if terms are OK.

Appreciate a tight summary we can push to Legal. Can we get a written proposal today/tomorrow and a 30-minute call early next week?

Regards,
Jane
Jane Doe
Procurement Manager, BlueWave Medical
jane.doe@bluewavemed.com
Attachments: BlueWave_SOW_20260520.pdf (pdf, 42KB)


From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
To: Jane Doe <jane.doe@bluewavemed.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 20 May 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: VPC pricing breakdown & support options

Jane —

Thanks — great to meet yesterday. I’m looping in Marissa (commercial lead) and Soojin (solutions/ops) so we can get a compact commercial package over to you.

High-level answers up front:
- We can amortize the platform fee across a 12-month schedule for pilots on request, or invoice it up front as a single platform add-on. We’ll show both options in the doc.
- License pricing (on-prem/VPC) is a perpetual license + annual maintenance model; platform fee covers control plane, telemetry, and console hosting (if selected) — we’ll break the typical split in USD and present per-node vs metered examples.
- Support: Standard (business hours), Premium (24/7 monitoring + 4hr response), and Enterprise (24/7 on-call with named on-call engineer and SLA-backed credits). We’ll include prices and a short SLA table.
- Pilot discount: we can offer a time-bound pilot concession. I’ll propose a 20% pilot discount on license fees for the first 3 months, plus a phased platform-fee amortization option.

I attached a draft term sheet and a short cost example. If that looks OK we’ll prepare a one-page SOW for your legal to review.

Proposed times for a 30′ call: Mon 5/25 at 10am PT or Tue 5/26 at 2pm PT. Which works?

Thanks,
Ethan Park
Head of Sales — Private Deployments
Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwood.com
Attachments: redwood_term_sheet_20260520.xlsx (xlsx, 18KB)


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 21 May 2026 09:30:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: BlueWave commercial options (draft)

Ethan / Soojin —

Drafting notes for the BlueWave response:

1) Price framing — be explicit on three billing profiles:
   - Upfront platform fee (one-time) + perpetual license (invoice upfront)
   - Platform fee amortized across 12 months + perpetual license invoice upfront
   - Fully metered model (lower platform fee, higher per-token/runtime charges) — include an example where BlueWave runs < 3 nodes average.

2) Pilot concession — suggest we offer a 20% license fee discount for first 90 days and waive first-month platform fee if they commit to a 6-month evaluation and provide a non-binding forecast. This is more flexible than yesterday’s off-the-cuff and helps Legal.

3) Support tiers — include a small matrix (response times, included hours, escalation path, named on-call for Enterprise). For on-call pricing: $12k/mo for 24/7 on-call + priority routing (negotiable).

4) PO timeline — include standard procurement steps and our SOW template link (we’ll use the redlined version from last quarter).

I’ll turn this into a single-page pricing appendix + SOW stub and send to Ethan to share. Thoughts on waiving the first-month platform fee vs offering a small credit on invoice? My leaning: waive to reduce friction for pilot sign-up.

—Marissa


From: Jane Doe <jane.doe@bluewavemed.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 22 May 2026 08:14:00 -0700
Subject: Re: VPC pricing breakdown, license fee split & support tiers

Ethan / Marissa —

Thanks — this is helpful. A few clarifications from procurement/legal:

- We prefer amortization of the platform fee across 12 months rather than a large upfront charge; please show exact monthly amortized numbers.
- For the pilot concession: can you confirm the license discount applies to the seat/node license only and not the platform fee? Procurement is OK with a waived first-month platform fee if you can keep the license discount to a maximum of 20% for the first 90 days.
- On-call pricing at $12k/mo is above our internal benchmark — can you provide a split (engineering hours included vs true 24/7 escalation) and whether we can instead purchase blocks of on-call hours at a lower rate (e.g., $3k per 40-hour block)?
- Please send the one-page SOW and a DocuSign URL for approval routing. Also confirm whether the platform telemetry data retention is 90 days by default (we need 365 days for audit) and whether that is an add-on.

If you can send an updated appendix and the SOW + DocuSign link by COB today we’ll have the procurement review on Monday.

Best,
Jane


From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
To: Jane Doe <jane.doe@bluewavemed.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 22 May 2026 15:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: VPC pricing breakdown, license fee split & support tiers

Jane —

Thanks for the quick clarifications. Finalizing and summarizing below (we’ll send the updated appendix + SOW + DocuSign link in a follow-up email):

- Platform fee amortization: approved — we will show a 12-month amortization schedule. Example: $120k platform fee = $10k/mo for 12 months (we will include a variant with a $60k platform fee if they pick metered model).

- Pilot concession: license discount = 20% maximum for first 90 days; platform fee: waive month 1 if BlueWave confirms a 6-month evaluation window in writing (non-binding forecast is fine). We will call this the \"Pilot Waiver\" in the appendix.

- On-call: we can offer two options: (A) Named 24/7 on-call at $12k/mo (full escalation path + named engineer) or (B) blocks of on-call hours at $3k per 40-hour block with 24/7 callback but not a dedicated named engineer. We’ll show both rates and an example costing for expected usage.

- Telemetry retention: default 90 days. 365-day retention is an add-on; we’ll price it as $X per TB/mo (we’ll include a conservative estimate for their expected volume).

Attachments in follow-up: pricing appendix (one page), one-page SOW (editable), DocuSign link.

If this summary looks correct I’ll send the formal appendix. We’re available to finalize the DocuSign bundle by Mon 5/25 at 10am PT.

Thanks,
Ethan Park
Head of Sales — Private Deployments
Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwood.com
Attachments (to follow): pricing_appendix_20260522.pdf, BlueWave_SOW_final_20260522.pdf

Hi Samantha / Oliver,

Following our playbook request, attached is a first-pass health & safety plan for the May 18–19 offsite in Boston. This focuses on guest wellbeing (onsite med kit, allergies, heat/workshop fatigue), local emergency contacts, and an evacuation/transport contingency if a guest needs urgent care.

High-level items in the attached checklist:
- Designated on-site first aid point and trained Redwood rep
- Central emergency contact list (hospital, security, transport vendor)
- Simple triage rules for deciding med-evac vs. on-site care
- Remote fallback plan: how we switch to fully remote facilitation within 60 minutes
- Access & badge flow for Acme security / building front desk coordination

title: Joint-branded SaaS integration roadmap and commercial next steps
preview:
From: Aria Johnson <aria@attune.ai>
To: Sergio Alvarez <sergio@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Levinson <markus@tethysco.com>
Date: Tue, 5 May 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Attune x Redwood — interest in embedding inference for our knowledge assistant

Hi Sergio,

Thanks again for the intro via Markus. Attune is piloting a customer-facing knowledge assistant and we're evaluating options to embed inference into our SaaS product under our brand (white-label).

High level: we need low-latency, predictable billing, and an option for on-prem for a few regulated customers. We liked Redwood's cost controls and rollout features from the benchmarks.

Can we do a short 30-min call to walk the product/ops/technical constraints and get a sense of commercial models (hosted metered vs reserved capacity vs private)?

Attached: a 1-pager of our intended UX and a short list of target SLAs.

Best,
Aria Johnson
Head of Product, Attune AI
aria@attune.ai

Attachment: attune-integration-1pager.pdf (pdf, 210KB)


From: Sergio Alvarez <sergio@redwood.com>
To: Aria Johnson <aria@attune.ai>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 5 May 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Attune x Redwood — interest in embedding inference for our knowledge assistant

Aria — great to meet you (virtually). Thanks for the 1-pager. Happy to run through the options and put together a rapid partnership plan.

Proposed agenda for a 30-min call:
- 10 min: Attune product goals + target customers (you)
- 10 min: Redwood integration patterns (hosted API, Dedicated pools, Private VPC) + tradeoffs
- 10 min: commercial models + next steps (MVP SOW, NDA, pilot timeline)

Availability: Wed 5/6 between 9–11am PT or Thu 5/7 after 1pm PT. What works for you?

Next steps from Redwood side: I'll loop in Priyom (SE) and Laura (Commercial) — Priyom can sketch technical constraints ahead of the call; Laura can outline commercial guardrails so the first call is productive.

Looking forward,
Sergio Alvarez
Head of Partnerships | Redwood Inference
sergio@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0198
Redwood Inference — Developer-first inference runtime


From: Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.com>
To: Aria Johnson <aria@attune.ai>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio@redwood.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 5 May 2026 12:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Attune x Redwood — interest in embedding inference for our knowledge assistant

Aria — quick note from the SE perspective before our call:

1) Integration patterns we typically see:
   - Client calls Redwood hosted API (streaming) with Attune UI shaping prompts. Fastest to ship, pay-as-you-go.
   - Dedicated pool: reserved GPU capacity for predictable throughput and pinning model versions (good for concierge SLAs).
   - Private deployment (VPC/on-prem): for regulated customers who need network isolation + customer-managed keys.

2) Technical questions that will affect architecture/pricing:
   - Expected QPS and 95th percentile latency SLO for the assistant?
   - Typical context window (token length) and percentage of cached prefixes (we can reduce tokens with prefix caching).
   - Any requirement for model persistence (pinning to a specific model version / quantized variant)?

3) For a white-label SDK surface: we can provide a small client library that wraps token usage accounting and supports branded response headers. That is usually an 2–4 week scope for an SDK + docs (SE + small eng effort).

I'll bring a short tech note and sample config (hosted vs dedicated) to share on the call.

—Priyom
Priyom Das | Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
priyom@redwood.com


From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Aria Johnson <aria@attune.ai>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Levinson <markus@tethysco.com>
Date: Wed, 6 May 2026 08:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Attune x Redwood — interest in embedding inference for our knowledge assistant

Hi Aria and team — thanks for looping us in. From a commercial perspective, a few quick items to align on:

- NDA: we usually sign a mutual NDA before sharing SOW templates and detailed benchmarks. Happy to send our standard mutual NDA (we can redline).
- Pricing guardrails: for an MVP pilot we typically propose a 3-month pilot on a metered hosted plan with a commitment threshold that can convert to a Dedicated offer if QPS and latency need it.
- White-label terms: we have a partner OEM clause that covers logo use, joint-branding, and permitted customer communications; we can surface that as part of the SOW.

I'll attach a short pilot commercial template (SOW + pricing bands). If you want, I can propose a pilot pricing example after we get rough QPS/usage numbers.

Best,
Laura Bennett
Director, Commercial Partnerships | Redwood Inference
