title: MAP & Evaluation Plan — timeline, commercial milestones, and SLA tradeoffs
preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@acuityhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, Apr 1, 2026 at 09:15 AM PDT
Subject: MAP: Evaluation plan & commercial milestones for Acuity POC

Hi Sarah —

Thanks again for the time today. Per our conversation, I attached a first-draft Mutual Action Plan (MAP) that lays out the evaluation milestones, success criteria, and the commercial milestones we discussed (commits, ramp, and proposed discount bands). Key highlights in the doc: 

- 2-week sandbox kick-off (data ingest + connectivity)
- 6-week POC/eval window with clear success criteria (latency targets on sample workloads, end-to-end throughput, and quality checks on 3 representative prompts)
- Commercial runway: optional 3-month ramp after POC where you can opt into reserved capacity pricing with tiered discounts at commit thresholds (see Pricing section)

To make the commercial offer straightforward: we can provide a conditional discount schedule tied to published MAP milestones — e.g., 10% off list when Acuity signs a 6-month committed throughput at >= X tokens/month after a successful POC, and an additional 15% if you meet the 3-month ramp targets by month 3. In exchange for the deeper discount, we ask for a modest uptime/SLA commitment (we’ll propose latency SLOs below).

On SLA & latency: for the POC we’ll guarantee service-level latency guidance (p95 and p99 on our hosted offering for the selected model). For commercial commits, we can formalize a 99th percentile latency SLO of 350ms for inference under the agreed traffic profile; if we miss that SLO in two consecutive months, we will provide service credits and a priority remediation path. If Acuity requests a tighter p99 (<= 250ms), that would need to be scoped to dedicated capacity (Reserved GPU pool) and would require a higher commit (see pricing table).

Please review the attached MAP and SOW draft. If you’re comfortable, can we lock the evaluation timeline this week and confirm a preferred model variant (cost vs latency tradeoff) so we can provision a sandbox?

Thanks —
Jordan Blake
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference
attachments: MAP_Acuity_V1.pdf, SOW_draft_0412.docx


From: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@acuityhealth.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Martha Gomez <martha.gomez@acuityhealth.com>, procurement@acuityhealth.com
Date: Thu, Apr 2, 2026 at 10:02 AM PDT
Subject: Re: MAP: Evaluation plan & commercial milestones for Acuity POC

Jordan —

Thanks for the MAP — this is helpful and aligns with what we discussed. A few quick notes from our side: 

- We want the sandbox to include an anonymized subset of production traffic and an integration checklist (auth + KV cache behavior) so our infra team can validate end-to-end latency.
- For success criteria, please add a quality gate: automated BLEU/ROUGE checks on the 3 core prompts + a manual review pass by our clinical content lead.
- On pricing: the discount tiers look reasonable, but procurement will need the commit thresholds spelled out numerically (tokens/month and minimum spend).
- Latency SLO: Acuity’s core UX requires p99 <= 300ms for top-line workflows. We’re open to dedicated capacity for those paths if you can map the cost delta.

We’ll review the SOW and follow up with procurement if there are redlines. If you can update the MAP with the addition above and a clear numerical commit table, I’ll run it by the exec sponsor (CPO) this week.

Best,
Sarah Lin
Sr. Product Manager, Automation
Acuity Health


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@acuityhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, Apr 12, 2026 at 11:08 AM PDT
Subject: Re: MAP: Evaluation plan & commercial milestones for Acuity POC

Sarah —

Appreciate the clear asks. I updated the MAP (attached) to include: sandbox dataset checklist, explicit quality gates (automated + manual), and a numerical commit table. High-level commercial proposal (summary):

- POC (6 weeks): Hosted sandbox free of charge; sample tokens capped at 500k tokens across the test set.
- Ramp option (3 months post-POC): If Acuity commits to a 6-month contract at >= 5M tokens/month, Redwood will apply a 10% baseline discount off hosted list pricing.
- Accelerated discount: If after 3 months into the ramp you are at >= 8M tokens/month sustained for a full month, we’ll add an incremental 15% (so total 25% off list) for the remainder of the 6-month commitment.
- SLA/latency tradeoff: For the baseline hosted commitment we’ll contract a p95 <= 220ms and p99 <= 350ms (measured on a representative 90th-percentile payload distribution you’ll approve). Missed SLO: one-time remediation window and service credits equal to 5% of that month’s invoice for each additional missed SLO, capped at 20% per quarter.
- Dedicated capacity option: For guaranteed p99 <= 250ms we'd propose Reserved Capacity (dedicated GPU pool). The cost delta is roughly +40% over the baseline committed price but would remove the monthly service credit exposure (instead we agree an operational SLA with 99.9% uptime and a 2-hour P1 response).

Notes on acceptance workflow: procurement will need a signed SOW/PO for the commit to trigger the discount. Legal will want the SLA language tightened (we can pre-fill standard remediation language). Marissa — tagging you since anything beyond 20% discount needs VP approval.

Attached: MAP_Acuity_V2.pdf (with commit table) and SOW_draft_0412.docx (updated).

Jordan Blake
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference
attachments: MAP_Acuity_V2.pdf, SOW_draft_0412.docx


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, Apr 13, 2026 at 09:34 AM PDT
Subject: Re: MAP: Evaluation plan & commercial milestones for Acuity POC

Jordan —

Thanks for the write-up and for flagging the discount bands. A couple items from GTM/finance/legal perspective: 

- Any total discount >20% requires VP approval; the ’10% then +15%’ stacking to 25% is fine to offer as conditional but we should mark the contract language as \"exec approval required\" and include a short justification (strategic account ramp + reference customer potential).
- SLA: the proposed remediation / credit structure is acceptable as a short-term commercial concession for the ramp, but we should ensure legal adds limitations of liability and force majeure carve-outs. For dedicated capacity, confirm if the customer needs a 90-day minimum lead time to spin-up GPUs in the region (this will affect the timeline).
- Please capture in the MAP a one-pager on acceptance criteria for latency testing (payload mix, synthetic vs production sampling) so Solutions can reproduce results. Ethan, can you prepare that?

If Acuity signs the SOW and procurement issues a PO by April 23, we can hit the sandbox start on Apr 27. Anything beyond that likely pushes dates into May.

Marissa Cole
VP, Sales — Enterprise
Redwood Inference

Date: Thu, Apr 15, 2026 at 04:42 PM PDT

Procurement expects to send a PO draft by Apr 20, and security will confirm SOC2 posture (they asked for a short summary — I opened a request and attached the SOC2_evidence_summary.pdf).

We accept the baseline latency SLOs for the POC (p95 <= 220ms, p99 <= 350ms). For the high-performance paths where we require p99 <= 250ms, we will evaluate the dedicated capacity option and revert with a decision during the ramp.

title: Network evidence request for SIG/SOC2 — Dedicated & Private architectures
preview:
From: James Ellis <james.ellis@acmepayments.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Cc: security-team@acmepayments.com
Date: 2026-04-11T16:12:00-07:00
Subject: Security evidence request — network topology and isolation for SIG/SOC2

Hi Rachel,

We're working through the SIG and SOC2 sections related to network controls for our upcoming vendor assessment. Can you help us by providing: 

- A redacted network topology diagram that covers both Redwood Dedicated and Redwood Private deployment models (we do not need exact CIDRs if redaction is required).
- A one-page isolation matrix describing how tenant/network isolation is enforced in each offering (control plane vs. data plane, scheduling/isolation guarantees).
- References to where VPC flow logs, firewall rules, and audit logs are retained (storage location, retention period) and whether they can be exported for auditor review.

If possible, please include a short statement on whether there is any cross-tenant data or control-plane traffic that could route through other customers' infrastructure. Our deadline for initial documentation is Apr 19.

Thanks — let me know if a redacted PDF is acceptable or if you require a different format.

Best,
James
James Ellis
Security Lead, Acme Payments
security-team@acmepayments.com

(P.S. attached is the questionnaire excerpt we are focussing on: SIG_section_network.pdf)

From: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
To: James Ellis <james.ellis@acmepayments.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-04-12T09:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Security evidence request — network topology and isolation for SIG/SOC2

Hi James —

Thanks for the clear ask. Redacted diagrams are fine; we typically provide a redacted topology PNG/PDF plus an isolation matrix spreadsheet for auditors. To confirm we will: 

- Produce a redacted topology that omits customer CIDRs but shows VPC boundaries, PrivateLink/peering paths, control-plane endpoints, and flow log aggregation endpoints.
- Provide an isolation matrix that maps deployment type -> network controls, host scheduling, key-management boundaries, and audit log retention.
- Include brief artifacts: sample VPC flow log export path, SMK/KMS CMK use for log encryption, and a short note on bastion/jump host usage.

Neha/Ethan: Ethan, can you assemble the redacted PNG + isolation_matrix. Neha, please confirm the retention period and SOC2 scope language (we default to 13 months for flow logs).

James — would an annotated PNG + XLSX be suitable for your auditor? If yes, we will send a link and attachments by EOD Apr 13.

Cheers,
Rachel Kim
Director, Sales Ops — Redwood Inference
rachel.kim@redwood.ai



From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-04-12T10:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Security evidence request — network topology and isolation for SIG/SOC2

Adding some draft notes for the artifacts we're preparing. Attached will be: redwood_vpc_topology_redacted.png and isolation_matrix_2026-04-12.xlsx. Quick implementation notes (internal summary we can include in the readme):

- Dedicated offering: single-tenant GPU pools are provisioned into isolated host pools. There is no cross-tenant VM scheduling at the hypervisor level; tenant compute is pinned to dedicated instances or GPU partitions. Network: dedicated VPC or isolated subnet pools; control plane endpoints are served over a separate, customer-dedicated control plane network where applicable.

- Private (VPC/On-prem): customers either peer their VPCs to Redwood control-plane endpoints using VPC Peering or PrivateLink. Data plane stays in customer's network or in a private VPC; control plane traffic uses mTLS-protected endpoints.

- Common controls: TLS 1.2+ (we are rolling out 1.3 where supported), mandatory mTLS for control plane tooling, VPC Flow Logs forwarded to a centralized S3 bucket (encrypted with a customer-specific KMS CMK for Private; Redwood-managed CMK for Hosted), retention default 13 months (configurable for enterprise + SOC addendum).

- Maintenance/bastion access: ephemeral bastions with session recording; access is timeboxed and requires 2FA and RBAC approval. No persistent admin SSH into tenant instances.

- Audit evidence: CloudTrail-equivalent logs, flow logs, and orchestration events are storable and exportable to the auditor under an NDA. We include a short README explaining redaction fields.

I'll attach the draft PNG/XLSX in the next message. Let me know if we should include architecture notes on SR-IOV and PCIe passthrough for the Dedicated model.

—Ethan
Ethan Park
Infrastructure Engineer, Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwood.ai



From: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
To: James Ellis <james.ellis@acmepayments.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-04-13T15:48:00-07:00
Subject: Delivering redacted network topology + isolation matrix

James —

Attached are the artifacts requested for the SIG/SOC2 evaluation:

- redwood_vpc_topology_redacted.png (annotated, shows Dedicated and Private high-level paths; all customer CIDRs removed)
- isolation_matrix_2026-04-12.xlsx (deployment rows and control columns; includes notes on host scheduling and KMS boundaries)
- redwood_network_evidence_readme.txt (short readme describing what was redacted, how to request non-redacted artifacts under NDA)

Summary (2 bullets):
- Dedicated: Single-tenant GPU pools; host-level isolation; no cross-tenant scheduling; control/data plane separation enforced at the network and orchestration layers.
- Private: Customer-managed VPC or on-prem network with PrivateLink/peering to Redwood control plane; control plane communicates over mTLS and customers retain VPC-level control of data plane egress.

Evidence notes: Flow logs and orchestration events are retained in our central storage (S3, encrypted with KMS). Default log retention is 13 months; we can set enterprise retention via contract. Bastion/jump sessions are ephemeral, recorded, and require RBAC approval.

Drive link to artifacts (secure): https://drive.redwood.com/s/secure/redacted-2026-04-13

If your auditor needs an unredacted topology (full CIDRs and instance IDs), we will require an executed NDA and scoped access. Otherwise these redacted artifacts should meet SIG/SOC2 evidence expectations.

If you want to schedule a 30-minute walkthrough with our Security team, I can loop Neha on a call next week.

Thanks,
Rachel Kim

- Dedicated offering: single-tenant GPU pools are provisioned into isolated host pools. There is no cross-tenant VM scheduling at the hypervisor level; tenant compute is pinned to dedicated instances or GPU partitions. Network: dedicated VPC or isolated subnet pools; control plane endpoints are served over a separate, customer-dedicated control plane network where applicable.

- Private (VPC/On-prem): customers either peer their VPCs to Redwood control-plane endpoints using VPC Peering or PrivateLink. Data plane stays in customer's network or in a private VPC; control plane traffic uses mTLS-protected endpoints.

- Common controls: TLS 1.2+ (we are rolling out 1.3 where supported), mandatory mTLS for control plane tooling, VPC Flow Logs forwarded to a centralized S3 bucket (encrypted with a customer-specific KMS CMK for Private; Redwood-managed CMK for Hosted), retention default 13 months (configurable for enterprise + SOC addendum).

- Maintenance/bastion access: ephemeral bastions with session recording; access is timeboxed and requires 2FA and RBAC approval. No persistent admin SSH into tenant instances.

- Audit evidence: CloudTrail-equivalent logs, flow logs, and orchestration events are storable and exportable to the auditor under an NDA. We include a short README explaining redaction fields.

title: Integrating Redwood with LangChain / LlamaIndex: adapter + tracing proposal
preview:
From: Aileen Park <aileen@searchbeam.ai>
To: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 5 Apr 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Redwood + LangChain/LlamaIndex integration?

Hi Jonas,

Quick intro — I'm Aileen, Head of Platform Partnerships at SearchBeam Labs. We're building a developer-focused vector search + tools product and are exploring Redwood as our preferred inference backend for a managed integration.

High-level asks:
- We want a LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter that can call Redwood endpoints with minimal glue (schema for embeddings, streaming responses, and tool-calling support).
- First-class tracing: we need per-request spans that capture prompt template ids, token counts, model variant used, and any fallback events (region/capacity).
- Prompt tooling hooks: ability to attach template metadata and a stable prompt id for downstream analytics.

Are these things Redwood supports today, or would we need an SDK adapter layer? Would love to set up a 30-min call next week. We're aiming to pilot end-to-end with a handful of customers in June.

Thanks,
Aileen
Aileen Park
Head of Platform Partnerships | SearchBeam Labs


From: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
To: Aileen Park <aileen@searchbeam.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 5 Apr 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Redwood + LangChain/LlamaIndex integration?

Hi Aileen — great to meet you, and thanks for outlining the use case. Redwood definitely sees this as a great partner fit.

Short answers:
- LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter: doable. We have an SDK that teams commonly wrap with a small adapter to map chain/agent calls to our text/generation and embedding endpoints.
- Tracing: we expose per-request telemetry and can emit OpenTelemetry-compatible spans. We also have structured metadata fields for prompt ids and token accounting.
- Prompt hooks: we can persist a prompt_id and expose it in traces and the Console for quality monitoring.

Next steps I propose:
1) Quick 30-min technical sync (Marissa + one of our infra engineers).
2) Draft a lightweight MLP (mutual action plan) with milestones for adapter, tracing integration, and pilot.
3) Exchange a sample LangChain/LlamaIndex agent config and expected QPS.

I'm free Tue/Wed next week (Apr 7–8) 10–2 PT. Does that work?

Regards,
Jonas Weber
Senior Strategic Partner Manager | Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 7 Apr 2026 10:20:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Redwood + LangChain/LlamaIndex integration?

Ethan — forwarding a partner ask from SearchBeam. They're asking for a LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter + tracing hooks and prompt ids. High-level requirements in original thread.

Key concerns we should evaluate before the call:
- KV cache interplay when they do stateful agents (prefix caching + streaming).
- How much per-request metadata we can expose without breaking throughput (serializing prompt templates into traces).
- Quantization/model variants: they may expect fallback behavior — how do we surface that in spans?

I've attached a short note with scope/questions (integration-scope-note.md). Can you review and propose who from infra/runtime should join the sync?

Thanks — Marissa

---- Forwarded message ----
From: Aileen Park <aileen@searchbeam.ai>
Date: Sun, 5 Apr 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Redwood + LangChain/LlamaIndex integration?
To: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>

[original message]


From: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
To: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Apr 2026 14:55:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Redwood + LangChain/LlamaIndex integration? — technical notes

Jonas / Marissa — read the scope note and sketched some technical constraints and a proposed adapter design below. High-level: doable with a 2–3 sprint eng effort for a first-pass adapter + tracing middleware.

Technical notes / proposal:
- Adapter: a thin client library that maps LangChain LLM / Tool calls to Redwood REST/SDK. Responsibilities:
  * Map LLM.generate / ChatChain -> text generation endpoint; support streaming and function/tool calling.
  * EmbedWriter adapter for LlamaIndex to call Redwood embeddings endpoint.
  * Provide a configuration option to pin model variants or allow automatic fallback.
- Tracing middleware:
  * Emit OpenTelemetry spans per request with tags: prompt_template_id (if provided), prompt_sha256, model_variant, token_input_count, token_output_count, fallback_reason (if any), region_used.
  * Sampling: default to sample 1% of requests; enable full traces for pilot customers or on-demand via SDK flag.
  * For streaming responses, emit a root span for the request and child spans for each batch/stream chunk if the user enables detailed tracing (opt-in).
- Prompt tooling hooks:
  * Allow callers to attach prompt metadata in a reserved header (X-Redwood-Prompt-Id) which will appear in traces and Console events.
  * Store a short mapping (prompt_id -> template) in Console for quality checks (TTL'd).
- Performance considerations:
  * Avoid serializing large templates into traces by storing a hash and small metadata.
  * Ensure KV cache keys are not included in traces; only include cache hit/miss booleans and size metrics.

Questions for SearchBeam (to include on the call):
- Expected QPS and average prompt length (tokens).
- Are they using LangChain agents with long-running memory/KV state?
- Telemetry ingestion: do they want traces sent to their OTLP collector or ours?

Suggested attendees from our side: infra runtime engineer (TBD), SDK lead (me), Marissa (partnership owner).

Ethan
Senior Software Engineer, Serving Runtime | Redwood Inference


From: Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.com>
To: Aileen Park <aileen@searchbeam.ai>, SearchBeam Labs <eng-partner@searchbeam.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 12 Apr 2026 16:43:00 -0700
Subject: Redwood <> SearchBeam: integration plan and next steps

Aileen — thanks again for the conversation and for walking through SearchBeam's goals. Per our follow-up, attaching a short proposal that lays out a pilot plan and timeline (redwood-searchbeam-adapter-proposal.pdf). Key points below: 

Summary of proposal:
- Scope: LangChain adapter + LlamaIndex embeddings connector + OpenTelemetry tracing middleware + prompt_id metadata plumbing.
- Pilot milestones (8 weeks):
  1) Week 1–2: Adapter prototype, basic mapping for generate/embeddings, sample notebook.
  2) Week 3–4: Tracing middleware, sample OTLP exporter, and Console visibility for prompt_id events.
  3) Week 5–8: Pilot with two SearchBeam customers, telemetry validation, cost/latency tuning (batching / cache).
- Deliverables: SDK adapter repo, integration tests, pilot runbook, post-pilot performance report.

Operational asks from SearchBeam:
- Provide sample LangChain/LlamaIndex configs and expected QPS by Apr 20.
- Decide whether traces should route to your OTLP collector or stay in Redwood (we support both).
- SOC2/security review: I'll open a ticket with our security team — please share the relevant contact.

Next steps:
- Please review the attached proposal and sign off on the pilot scope by Apr 20 if the timeline works.
- We'll schedule a technical kickoff with Ethan and our infra engineer (I will propose times tomorrow).

Looking forward — excited to move this forward.

Best,
Jonas Weber
Senior Strategic Partner Manager | Redwood Inference
Attachments: redwood-searchbeam-adapter-proposal.pdf (application/pdf)

title: Intermittent 504s on /v1/generate — initial triage
preview:
From: Maya Roy <maya@starlite-health.com>
To: support@redwood.ai
Date: Fri, 10 Apr 2026 18:45:00 -0700
Subject: Intermittent 504s when calling /v1/generate (prod)

Hi Redwood team,

We're seeing intermittent 504s from our production app when hitting your /v1/generate endpoint. This started happening last night and has impacted about ~3% of requests between 2026-04-10T00:30:00Z and 2026-04-10T03:10:00Z. Attached are a screenshot of the browser error and a small text file with the request_ids and timestamps we captured from our logs.

Example request_id: req_20260410_1632Z_7f9a
We get a 504 Gateway Timeout from your side; upstream (our end) shows the request left our proxy and we see no evidence of a retry. Can you confirm there's no ongoing incident? This is impacting a small set of users for our scheduling flow.

Thanks — happy to jump on a quick call.

—
Maya Roy
Director of Product, Starlite Health
maya@starlite-health.com
+1 415 555 0193

Attachments: screenshot-504.png, starlite_request_ids_04102026.txt

From: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.ai>
To: Maya Roy <maya@starlite-health.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 10 Apr 2026 19:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent 504s when calling /v1/generate (prod)

Hi Maya — thanks for the report and the logs. I'm Diego on the Customer Success/Sales side, looping in Ethan (support) and Marissa (CS) to triage quickly.

Quick questions to help prioritize:
- Are these requests against our hosted Redwood API or Dedicated capacity? (I see your account points to hosted but want to confirm)
- Can you share a full HAR or the upstream CDN/provider (if any) so we can rule out a middlebox?
- Are the request_ids in the attached file the canonical set or did you capture more? If possible, include a couple of full request/response headers.

We'll start by checking runtime logs and request traces for the timestamps you gave. Ethan — can you take point on reproduction and runtime checks? Marissa — if this is impacting SLAs, can you open/support the HubSpot ticket (HS-96481) and coordinate updates?

Maya — we'll get back in <4 hours with initial findings. If it's easier to call, I can make time this afternoon.

Best,
Diego
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
diego.fernandez@redwood.ai | +1 650 555 0122

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
To: Maya Roy <maya@starlite-health.com>
Cc: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 10 Apr 2026 22:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent 504s when calling /v1/generate (prod) — engineering triage notes

Hi Maya, Diego, Marissa — Ethan here from Support/Infra. I ran reproduction tests using the sample payloads that match the shape and token counts you provided and exercised the /v1/generate endpoint repeatedly against our hosted fleet. Summary of findings below: 

What we checked:
- Agent-side reproduction: replayed 1,200 requests with the same headers/body; no 504s observed locally.
- Runtime traces: examined request traces for the timestamp window 2026-04-10T00:30:00Z to 2026-04-10T03:10:00Z for your request_ids. We found the matched request_ids in our logs; those requests completed with 200 responses on our side.
- Load & capacity: no cluster-wide resource pressure or autoscaler events in that window.
- Proxy/gateway: no upstream gateway error spikes, but I did observe a small cluster of TCP retransmit events from one edge node between 2026-04-10T01:42:00Z and 2026-04-10T01:48:00Z — this can correlate with transient connectivity issues between our edge and your CDN/provider.
- KV/cache eviction: normal. No errors.

Attachments: I am attaching an internal trace snippet that shows the server-side handling for the request_ids you provided (engineer-trace-04111.log). Note these are sanitized traces (no PII).

Preliminary conclusion:
- Based on server-side logs and reproduction attempts, we do not see an internal server error or model hang that would produce persistent 504s. The entries for your request_ids show 200s on our side. The most likely explanation is a transient network/connectivity issue between an edge node and your CDN/proxy (or a short-lived intermediary failure).

Next steps / ask from you:
- Can you share a HAR (or the upstream proxy logs) for one of the failing requests so we can see the exact timing and any intermediate 502/504 handed back by a CDN?

Hi Maya, Diego, Marissa — Ethan here from Support/Infra. I ran reproduction tests using the sample payloads that match the shape and token counts you provided and exercised the /v1/generate endpoint repeatedly against our hosted fleet. Summary of findings below: 

What we checked:
- Agent-side reproduction: replayed 1,200 requests with the same headers/body; no 504s observed locally.
- Runtime traces: examined request traces for the timestamp window 2026-04-10T00:30:00Z to 2026-04-10T03:10:00Z for your request_ids. We found the matched request_ids in our logs; those requests completed with 200 responses on our side.
- Load & capacity: no cluster-wide resource pressure or autoscaler events in that window.
- Proxy/gateway: no upstream gateway error spikes, but I did observe a small cluster of TCP retransmit events from one edge node between 2026-04-10T01:42:00Z and 2026-04-10T01:48:00Z — this can correlate with transient connectivity issues between our edge and your CDN/provider.
- KV/cache eviction: normal. No errors.

Attachments: I am attaching an internal trace snippet that shows the server-side handling for the request_ids you provided (engineer-trace-04111.log). Note these are sanitized traces (no PII).

Preliminary conclusion:
- Based on server-side logs and reproduction attempts, we do not see an internal server error or model hang that would produce persistent 504s. The entries for your request_ids show 200s on our side. The most likely explanation is a transient network/connectivity issue between an edge node and your CDN/proxy (or a short-lived intermediary failure).

- Can you share a HAR (or the upstream proxy logs) for one of the failing requests so we can see the exact timing and any intermediate 502/504 handed back by a CDN?

title: Acme Renewal: Proposal to increase Dedicated GPUs (16→32)
preview:
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Sarah James <sarah.james@acme-corp.com>, Alex Chen <alex.chen@acme-corp.com>
Cc: procure@acme-corp.com
Date: Sun, 1 Mar 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Acme Renewal: Proposal to increase Dedicated GPUs (16→32)

Hi Sarah / Alex,

Thanks again for the detailed usage data you shared last quarter. Based on the growth patterns and your SLA targets, we put together a renewal proposal that moves Acme from a 16-GPU reserved pool to a 32-GPU reserved pool for the next 12-month term. High-level changes: 
- Reserved capacity: 16 GPUs -> 32 GPUs (same instance family)
- Throughput guarantee: baseline throughput increased from 3.6k tokens/s to 7.2k tokens/s across the pool (measured at 95th percentile)
- Pricing: revised reserved hourly rate with tiered discount; see attached pricing_breakdown.xlsx and Acme_Renewal_Proposal_v3.pdf for line items
- Term: 12 months, auto-renew unless notified 60 days before expiry

We can offer a 12% effective discount on the additional 16 GPUs if you commit at signature, or a 9% discount if you prefer a change-of-term after renewal. Also included: automated fallback to smaller model variants for non-critical workloads, and one canary rollover during the first 30 days.

Next steps I suggest:
1) If the capacity and pricing look reasonable, let us know and we will generate a redline for legal + procurement.
2) If engineering needs a verification run, we can schedule a 2-hour performance validation with our infra team next week.

I attached the proposal and a short pricing matrix. Let me know which procurement contact to loop into the DocuSign.

Thanks,
Marissa
Marissa Cole | Account Executive, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0123

Attachments: Acme_Renewal_Proposal_v3.pdf (proposal), pricing_breakdown.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Alex Chen <alex.chen@acme-corp.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Sarah James <sarah.james@acme-corp.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Mar 2026 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Acme Renewal: Proposal to increase Dedicated GPUs (16→32)

Thanks Marissa — appreciate the proposal. A few quick clarifying questions from our side before we loop in procurement:

1) The throughput guarantee: can you confirm whether the 7.2k tokens/s is across the whole pool, or per-instance? We need pool-level guarantees for our SLOs.
2) If we commit now for the 12% discount, can we front-load some of the capacity in month 1? We expect a spike during our seasonal campaign in April.
3) Any changes to SLAs (uptime, credit calculations) compared to our current agreement?

CCing Sarah for procurement visibility. Once we have answers on these, I can pull in procure@acme-corp.com and legal for the PO and contract redline.

- Alex
Engineering Manager, Infrastructure
Acme Corp


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 3 Mar 2026 08:15:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Acme Renewal: Proposal to increase Dedicated GPUs (16→32)

Ethan / Vivek — forwarding Acme's clarifying questions. They want: pool-level throughput confirmation, ability to front-load capacity at month 1 for a campaign, and SLA delta info. From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 3 Mar 2026 11:48:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Fwd: Acme Renewal: Proposal to increase Dedicated GPUs (16→32)

Short answers:
- 7.2k tokens/s is achievable as a pool-level 95th pct for the 32-GPU reserved pool, assuming workload mix matches the telemetry Acme sent (mix: 55% short-chat prompts, 35% moderate seqs, 10% long-run batches). We will include a 5% headroom buffer in our SLA calculations.
- Front-loading: we can provision burst capacity in month 1, but full warm-up for KV/prefix caching requires a 48-hour verification run to hit steady-state latency. We recommend the 2-hour performance validation followed by a 48-hour scripted warm-up once provisioned.
- SLA changes: uptime SLA remains 99.9% for Dedicated; credit calculation identical to current contract. Throughput guarantee will be added as a new metric with a credit tier (up to 10% of monthly fees) if 95th-pt throughput falls short in 2 consecutive weeks.

- Reserved capacity: 16 GPUs -> 32 GPUs (same instance family)
- Throughput guarantee: baseline throughput increased from 3.6k tokens/s to 7.2k tokens/s across the pool (measured at 95th percentile)
- Pricing: revised reserved hourly rate with tiered discount; see attached pricing_breakdown.xlsx and Acme_Renewal_Proposal_v3.pdf for line items
- Term: 12 months, auto-renew unless notified 60 days before expiry

We can offer a 12% effective discount on the additional 16 GPUs if you commit at signature, or a 9% discount if you prefer a change-of-term after renewal. Also included: automated fallback to smaller model variants for non-critical workloads, and one canary rollover during the first 30 days.

Next steps I suggest:
1) If the capacity and pricing look reasonable, let us know and we will generate a redline for legal + procurement.
2) If engineering needs a verification run, we can schedule a 2-hour performance validation with our infra team next week.

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>

Cc: 
Date: Tue, 03 Mar 2026 16:12:00 -0700
Subject: POC kickoff — proposed slots & agenda (prompt review, routing, load tests)

Hi Elena / Carlos,

Thanks again for taking the time to evaluate Redwood. We're excited to run the POC and get hands-on with prompt review, routing policies, and a short load test to validate latency and token budgets.

Two quick questions up-front: who from your engineering team will join (name + email), and do you prefer Hosted trial or a short Dedicated eval for load testing? We can support both but want to size the capacity correctly.

Proposed kickoff windows (all ET):
- Thu Mar 5, 10:00–12:00 ET (preferred for internal alignment)
- Fri Mar 6, 09:00–11:00 ET (alternative)

Draft agenda (attached): 1) 20m intro & success criteria, 2) 40m prompt review + routing config, 3) 15m short load test (sustained burst 10–15m), 4) 15m capacity planning and next steps.

Attachments: POC_agenda_v1.pdf (agenda + checklist)

If those windows don't work, send a couple of alternatives and we'll reschedule. If you confirm the Thu slot I'll send a calendar invite and request a small quota bump (if you opt for Hosted) or a temporary Dedicated pool config.

Thanks,
Marissa Cole
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
marissa@redwood.com | (415) 555-0123

For context, our engineering lead (Carlos Mendez) will join, and I may loop in product (I'll confirm). A couple of quick asks so your team can size the POC:
- We have an approximate token budget: 120M tokens over 2 weeks during the POC peak.
- Can you outline the difference in behavior or constraints between Hosted trial and a Dedicated trial for sustained load? Specifically: rate limits, token quotas, and whether KV cache is preserved during Dedicated tests.

Carlos and I are flexible on tooling; we want to see prompt routing behavior and a short ramp in the load test.

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 04 Mar 2026 11:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: POC kickoff — capacity notes

Marissa / team — quick technical notes on sizing and behavior: 

1) Hosted vs Dedicated (summary):
- Hosted: best for fast onboarding; default rate limits and token quotas apply. We can request a temporary quota increase for a short, time-boxed load window but sustained high throughput can be noisy for multi-tenant pools.
- Dedicated: provides isolated GPU pools (predictable latency), persistent KV cache per dedicated endpoint, and configurable rate limits. Better if they want repeatable load runs and cache preservation across sessions.

2) For NovaProd's stated ~120M tokens over 2 weeks:
- If they run peak bursts during the POC, recommend Dedicated small pool for the 2-hour working session (1–2 A100-equivalent VMs or the nearest SKU we map), then scale to a small hosted burst for lower-volume interactions.
- Quota suggestion: request a Hosted tokens/min bump to 20k tokens/min for the short ramp; for Dedicated, provision a per-endpoint limit of 50k tokens/min across the pool and cap per-connection to 10k tokens/min to avoid head-of-line issues.

3) Load-test specifics: keep initial ramp 0->50% in 5 minutes, 50%->100% over next 10 minutes, sustain for 15 minutes, then cool down. Capture token rate, p50/p95 latency, and error rate.

Attaching quick capacity options file for ops: capacity-options.txt

I can stand up the Dedicated endpoint config (temporary) if CS approves the MMP and Finance signs off on the trial terms.

—Rishi
Rishi Malhotra
SRE / Inference Ops | Redwood Inference

preview:
From: Alex Rivera <alex@starlabs.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-02-20T15:12:00-05:00
Subject: Quickstart -> interested in a technical walkthrough

Hi Marissa,

I just finished the developer quickstart linked from the docs (great work — the guide is very clear). We have a small engineering team exploring how to adopt Redwood for inference behind a product feature (low-latency chat, embeddings for search). I wanted to see if we could set up a technical demo focused on integration patterns, batching/latency tradeoffs, and recommended quantization/instance types.

A few quick notes about our setup:
- Small infra team (2 engineers) on AWS us-west-2
- Prototype uses ~2k daily active users, expect burstiness
- We're comfortable running private deployments if needed

Can you recommend next steps or schedule an engineer-led demo? Prefer afternoons PST.

Thanks,
Alex Rivera
Senior Engineer, Starlabs
alex@starlabs.ai

title: Platform changes needed for SDK error consistency (Retry-After, request_id, error codes)
preview:
From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Platform changes needed for SDK error consistency (Retry-After, request_id, error codes)

Monica (cc Nikhil) —

As part of the SDK parity work, we need a couple small gateway/platform tweaks so Python/TS/Go can classify failures consistently and respect server guidance.

Proposed platform deltas (all additive / backwards-compatible):

1) Always include Retry-After on 429
- Today 429s are sometimes missing Retry-After depending on the route/downstream.
- SDK standard is: if Retry-After is present, it overrides computed backoff.
- Target: 429 responses should consistently include Retry-After (seconds format; optionally date format if upstream provides).

2) request_id propagation
- SDK conformance requires request_id be present on *all* error responses, including streaming disconnect/error cases.
- Today some streaming paths only have x-request-id header but not in JSON error body (and sometimes not surfaced when the connection dies mid-stream).
- Target: x-request-id always set; JSON error body includes request_id when applicable.

3) Stable error code field for SDK consumption
- We’ve had customers hit “same status code, different semantics” across endpoints.
- Target: error.code is from a documented enum (e.g., rate_limit_exceeded, overloaded, invalid_request, auth_failed), and SDKs can map that to retryable vs terminal.

I have three small PRs ready for review:
- redwood/pr-28411 (Retry-After on 429)
- redwood/pr-28426 (request_id propagation)
- redwood/pr-28440 (error code enum)

Timing request:
- SDK beta releases are slated to start end of next week. If we can land these and get them into prod behind normal rollout by ~Feb 24, it would reduce “works in TS but not Python” cases materially.

Can you confirm if Product/DevEx is OK with the behavior being default-on (again, additive fields/headers only), and if you want us to gate anything behind a feature flag?

Thanks,
Ethan Park
Platform / Control Plane
Redwood Inference


---

From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 14, 2026 at 10:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: Platform changes needed for SDK error consistency (Retry-After, request_id, error codes)

This all sounds directionally right.

Two questions before we bless “default on”:

1) Any risk that adding Retry-After will increase client retry behavior (and thus load) in a way SRE will hate? (I know SDK standard says “respect it”, but I want to make sure we don’t accidentally encourage faster retries.)

2) For request_id in streaming: do we have a plan for the “socket dies mid-chunk” scenario where there isn’t a final JSON body? As long as SDKs can still pull x-request-id early and surface it, we’re fine, but we should be explicit.

If the PRs are strictly additive and don’t change status codes/bodies beyond adding fields/headers, I’m OK not gating.

Do you have an estimated rollout plan (staging -> canary -> full) and who’s on point for any rollback?

- Monica
Monica Patel
Director, SDK Engineering


---

From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 14, 2026 at 11:20 AM PST
Subject: Re: Platform changes needed for SDK error consistency (Retry-After, request_id, error codes)

+1 from oncall side.

Context: we just had a customer escalation where the Go SDK ignored Retry-After because it wasn’t present (SUP-29477). The customer’s proxy + retries created a mini storm (not catastrophic, but noisy).

If the gateway always sends Retry-After on 429, and SDKs follow it, that actually should *reduce* the thundering herd (longer waits vs immediate exponential backoff starting too low).

Re: rollout/rollback: happy to have oncall monitor error rates + 429s during deploy. We should also watch for any endpoints where upstream already sets Retry-After in a weird format.

-Nikhil


---

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 15, 2026 at 2:41 PM PST
Subject: Re: Platform changes needed for SDK error consistency (Retry-After, request_id, error codes)

Answering inline.

On Sat, Feb 14, 2026 at 10:03 AM Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> 1) Any risk that adding Retry-After will increase client retry behavior (and thus load) in a way SRE will hate?

It should be the opposite in practice:
- For 429, Retry-After will generally be >= the current “first retry” SDK backoff.
- Clients that already retry will wait longer/more uniformly.
- Clients that don’t retry today won’t suddenly start because we’re not changing status code or adding any redirect-like behavior.

We’ll still coordinate with SRE for monitoring during rollout (Nikhil’s note aligns with what we’ve seen).

> 2) For request_id in streaming: do we have a plan for the “socket dies mid-chunk” scenario?

Yes. Two parts:
- Gateway will flush x-request-id header immediately on response start for streaming routes (already mostly true). PR-28426 tightens consistency.
- When the server terminates the stream with a structured error event (SSE error frame), we include request_id in that payload.

For the “hard disconnect mid-frame” case, there won’t be a final JSON body. SDKs will surface:
- request_id from the initial headers (if received)
- plus a standardized streaming_disconnect_reason (e.g., connection_reset, eof, timeout)

> estimated rollout plan and who’s on point for rollback?

Plan (tentative):
- Merge by Tue (2/17)
- Deploy to staging same day
- Canary (5%) Wed morning
- Full rollout Wed afternoon / Thu AM if metrics are stable

Rollback: platform oncall owns; changes are additive so rollback trigger would be unexpected increase in 5xx or malformed headers. Nikhil offered to monitor during the window.

Also: PR-28440 (error code enum) only adds/normalizes error.code values; we are NOT removing any existing error.message/error.type fields.

If you’re OK, I’ll ask for your review on the PRs and then queue for the next gateway release train.

-Ethan


---

From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 17, 2026 at 9:08 AM PST
Subject: Re: Platform changes needed for SDK error consistency (Retry-After, request_id, error codes)

Approved in principle.

Two asks before you merge:

- Please add/confirm a small integration test (or at least a staging smoke check checklist) that asserts:
  - 429 includes Retry-After
  - request_id is present in header and error body where applicable
  - error.code is present and from the enum on the common failure modes

- For PR-28440, can you ensure any legacy error.code values are still accepted (i.e., we normalize but don’t break dashboards/alerts that key off older strings)? If we’re changing strings, we should put a short note in release notes.

After merge, I’ll sync the SDK team so conformance tests can start depending on these fields.

- Monica


---

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 17, 2026 at 4:05 PM PST
Subject: Re: Platform changes needed for SDK error consistency (Retry-After, request_id, error codes)

Done.

- Added staging smoke checklist to the gateway deploy doc + a basic integration test for the 429 path (verifies Retry-After + request_id header).
- For error.code: we’re normalizing to the enum but keeping legacy strings as aliases for now (mapping layer). Release note added to the gateway changelog so downstream dashboards don’t get surprised.

Status:
- PR-28411 merged
- PR-28426 merged
- PR-28440 merged

Rollout:
- Staging tonight
- Canary tomorrow AM
- Full rollout pending metrics (Nikhil, I’ll ping you when canary starts so oncall can watch 429/5xx + any header parsing weirdness)

Monica: once canary is clean, feel free to have the SDK conformance suite assume Retry-After + request_id behavior.

Thanks both,
Ethan

title: Fwd: RRB-17: rollback loop in private installer (root cause + patch plan)
preview:
From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 13, 2026 at 7:15 AM
Subject: RRB-17: rollback loop in private installer (root cause + patch plan)

Root cause (RRB-17): when CRD migration partially applies, the rollback cleanup hook exits non-zero before clearing `installer-rollback-lock`. The controller treats rollback as failed and schedules another rollback attempt, which sees the lock and repeats. Net effect: "stuck rollback" / infinite rollback retries.

Patch plan:
- In rollback cleanup: clear lock in a `defer`-style best-effort step even if migration revert fails.
- Add lock TTL (annotation with timestamp) and refuse to retry rollback if lock older than N minutes; surface actionable status.
- Add metric `installer.rollback.loop_detected` + alert when >3 retries in 10m.

Workaround (current): delete CM `installer-rollback-lock` and restart installer-controller.

ETA: PR today, release in 2.9.1 if we can get a quick validation run.

---

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 13, 2026 at 8:03 AM
Subject: Re: RRB-17: rollback loop in private installer (root cause + patch plan)

+1. Please link the PR in INT-330 once up. Also can we update the upgrade runbook today with the safe command sequence + explicit warnings?

---

From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>

text:
From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 13, 2026 at 7:15 AM
Subject: RRB-17: rollback loop in private installer (root cause + patch plan)

Root cause (RRB-17): when CRD migration partially applies, the rollback cleanup hook exits non-zero before clearing `installer-rollback-lock`. The controller treats rollback as failed and schedules another rollback attempt, which sees the lock and repeats. Net effect: "stuck rollback" / infinite rollback retries.

Patch plan:
- In rollback cleanup: clear lock in a `defer`-style best-effort step even if migration revert fails.
- Add lock TTL (annotation with timestamp) and refuse to retry rollback if lock older than N minutes; surface actionable status.
- Add metric `installer.rollback.loop_detected` + alert when >3 retries in 10m.

Workaround (current): delete CM `installer-rollback-lock` and restart installer-controller.

ETA: PR today, release in 2.9.1 if we can get a quick validation run.

---

Subject: Short-term credits for stress/load validation

Hi Connor,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the Redwood stack performed well in our functional checks. We're lining up a POC stress run next Tuesday to validate latency and throughput at 2–3x expected production concurrency. Our internal test plan (attached) outlines 48 hours of continuous traffic with intermittent spike windows.

We're currently near our trial credit cap and will exhaust tokens if we drive the load we need. Would Redwood be able to provide a promotional credit top-up (one-time) to cover the load test? We're estimating ~100k token-equivalent credits based on the attached concurrency matrix, but we can be flexible if you prefer a smaller uplift and incremental top-ups.

If approved, we also need a brief note for procurement confirming this is a promotional, non-billable top-up tied to DL-00492.

Test artifacts: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1a2b3c-acme-poc-plan (acme_poc_testplan.pdf attached)

Acme IoT

Appreciate the detailed plan and drive. I reviewed the test matrix and have a few quick clarifying Qs before I escalate for a promotional top-up:

- Are you planning continuous streaming generation or batched requests during the 48h window? (affects token burn model)
- Do you require sustained concurrency at 2–3x, or are the spikes the primary concern?
- Is the traffic fingerprinting (IPs/regions) something we should whitelist ahead of time?

Priya: can you review the attached concurrency matrix and confirm whether our automatic batching/prefix cache will be effective for their workload or if we should recommend a temporary config for the run?

Ethan: if we approve a 100k credit uplift, we'll need a quick action plan for quota application and a backstop rollback. Can you prepare the operational steps (apply credit, monitor burn rate, throttle policy) so we can present both to Acme and to Marissa for approvals?

Sasha — if you can answer the above 1–2 bullets, I'll put this in front of Marissa for a fast-track decision. We aim to have credits in place 24 hours before your run.

Thanks,
Connor
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Ask: approve a 100,000 token-equivalent promotional credit (non-billable) to be applied to their trial project for a 7-day window, to ensure they can run the stress tests without space constraints. - Apply credit bundle to Acme trial project ID (will share ID once provisioned)

- Revoke remaining promo credits after 7 days, doc both parties

Related links: https://app.hubspot.com/deal/987654321/DL-00492
Attachments: acme_poc_testplan.pdf, concurrency_matrix.csv

If you can greenlight, I'll confirm with Acme and execute the action plan.

—Connor

title: Re: Urgent: Redwood Private upgrade keeps rolling back
preview:
From: Nina Shah <nina.shah@acme-example.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 12, 2026 at 9:43 AM
Subject: Urgent: Redwood Private upgrade keeps rolling back

Hi Ava,

We attempted the 2.8.0 -> 2.9.0 upgrade last night. Its been "rolling back" for hours and never completes. The UI now says "Rollback in progress" indefinitely.

Can you advise? We can share logs.

Nina

---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nina Shah <nina.shah@acme-example.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 12, 2026 at 10:01 AM
Subject: Re: Urgent: Redwood Private upgrade keeps rolling back

Thanks Nina  were looking. Can you attach the installer controller logs around the rollback start?

In the meantime: are you seeing any mention of a "rollback lock" or repeated rollback retries?

Ava

---

From: Nina Shah <nina.shah@acme-example.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 12, 2026 at 10:22 AM
Subject: Re: Urgent: Redwood Private upgrade keeps rolling back

Attached installer-controller-logs.txt. There are repeated lines like: "scheduling rollback retry" and it never clears.

---

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 12, 2026 at 10:48 AM
Subject: Re: Urgent: Redwood Private upgrade keeps rolling back

This matches the stuck rollback / rollback loop we saw (likely RRB-17). Workaround: delete CM `installer-rollback-lock` in the redwood-private namespace, then restart installer-controller. Id recommend we share steps with guardrails + ask them to confirm name/namespace before deleting.

---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nina Shah <nina.shah@acme-example.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 12, 2026 at 11:08 AM
Subject: Re: Urgent: Redwood Private upgrade keeps rolling back

Nina  we have a workaround that has unblocked this in our lab. If youre comfortable running a couple k8s commands, please do the following (and confirm youre in the *redwood-private* namespace):

1) `kubectl -n redwood-private delete cm installer-rollback-lock`
2) `kubectl -n redwood-private rollout restart deploy/installer-controller`

This should allow the rollback to complete. Please reply with the outcome + any remaining errors.

We are also tracking a patch to prevent the rollback loop from happening again.

Ava Chen
Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference

title: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients (Northwind AI)
preview:
From: Alyssa Kim <alyssa.kim@northwind.ai>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 9:14 AM PST
Subject: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients

Hi Nikhil,

We’re escalating an incident we’ve been dealing with this week.

Symptoms:
- When Redwood returns 429s, our client traffic spikes instead of backing off.
- We see bursts of retries from our TS services even when the 429 includes a message about rate limits.
- Our Go service appears to retry immediately (no visible respect for Retry-After).

Impact:
- Elevated latency and occasional request failures.
- Concern that retries are amplifying load (and in a few cases we saw duplicate requests close together, which is scary for our usage reporting).

Can you confirm what the expected SDK behavior is on 429, and whether the Redwood gateway returns Retry-After consistently?

We can hop on a call today if needed.

Thanks,
Alyssa

—
Alyssa Kim
Staff Engineer, Platform
Northwind AI



From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alyssa Kim <alyssa.kim@northwind.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 9:26 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients

Alyssa — thanks for flagging and sorry you’re dealing with this.

Looping in Monica (SDK Engineering) + Ethan (Platform) so we can get you a definitive answer + next steps.

A couple quick questions to help us root-cause:
1) Which SDK(s) + versions are you running (TS / Go / Python)?
2) Do you have any example request_id values from the 429 responses?
3) Are you using streaming or non-streaming endpoints for the bulk of these requests?

Expected behavior (high level): 429 should be retryable, but the SDK should respect Retry-After (if present) and apply exponential backoff with jitter + a max attempt cap. If you’re seeing immediate tight-loop retries, that’s not what we want.

If you can share a small log snippet + timestamps (and request_ids), we’ll correlate on our side.

Nikhil

—
Nikhil Sharma
Customer Engineering
Redwood Inference



From: Monica Patel <monica@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Alyssa Kim <alyssa.kim@northwind.ai>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 9:58 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients

Thanks Alyssa.

Context: we’re mid-flight on a cross-language SDK parity initiative specifically covering 429/Retry-After, timeouts, and retry/backoff consistency across TS/Go/Python.

What we believe is happening (based on similar support cases):
- Go SDK currently treats 429 as retryable but does not consistently parse/obey Retry-After (so it can retry faster than intended).
- TS SDK has retry middleware but we’ve found edge cases where the “retry delay” is not applied uniformly across request types (esp. when the underlying fetch/AbortController path is used).
- Gateway Retry-After header presence has historically not been 100% consistent on 429. Platform has a change in progress to standardize this.

Immediate mitigations you can apply today (if you need to stop the bleeding):
- Temporarily reduce max_retries (or disable retries) for 429 while you’re at/near rate limit.
- If you can, implement a global limiter on your side and treat 429 as a hard signal to back off.

Alyssa: if you can send us 2–3 request_ids and the SDK versions, we can confirm whether this matches the known gap and give you the exact patch version/ETA.

Monica

—
Monica Patel
Director, SDK Engineering
Redwood Inference



From: Ethan Park <ethan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Alyssa Kim <alyssa.kim@northwind.ai>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 10:12 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients

Adding platform detail:

We have a gateway PR in progress to ensure Retry-After is consistently emitted on 429 across all paths (including OpenAI-compat). There were a couple cases where a 429 was returned with a JSON error body but no Retry-After header.

Once that’s merged/rolled out, SDKs will have a reliable signal to sleep the appropriate amount.

Alyssa — if you can provide a sample of raw response headers for a 429 (or a HAR capture), we can confirm whether you’re hitting one of those paths.

Ethan

—
Ethan Park
Platform Engineering
Redwood Inference



From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alyssa Kim <alyssa.kim@northwind.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 10:29 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients

Alyssa,

Here’s what we’ll do next:

1) Confirm gateway behavior on your specific 429s (Retry-After present? seconds vs HTTP-date format?).
2) Confirm which SDK path is producing tight-loop retries.
3) Provide either a short-term config workaround + an ETA for the patch release.

If you can send:
- SDK versions for TS and Go
- 2–3 request_id examples
- A HAR / response headers for one 429

…we’ll start correlating immediately.

Also, to your duplicate request concern: our default guidance is to be conservative with retries for non-idempotent workflows. For text generation requests, retries can be safe but we recommend logging request_id and (if you have side effects) using an idempotency key pattern. We can share a recommended snippet after we see your exact usage.

Thanks,
Nikhil



From: Ravi Desai <ravi.desai@northwind.ai>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Alyssa Kim <alyssa.kim@northwind.ai>, Monica Patel <monica@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 12:03 PM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients

Jumping in with details.

SDK versions:
- redwood-sdk-typescript: 2.18.0
- redwood-sdk-go: 0.9.4

Workload is mostly non-streaming chat completions, with ~15% streaming.

Example request_ids from 429 responses:
- rw_req_01HTZVY4JH0X6P2D7R1B3MZB4Q
- rw_req_01HTZVZ0A3P9K3VY2C1G9Q2Y8N
- rw_req_01HTZW1K8J5B9Q8D0Q4S6D8W1P

Attached:
- northwind-prod-429-sample-logs.txt (shows retry loop every ~200–400ms)
- northwind-har-2026-02-05.json (one 429 response; I don’t see Retry-After header)

Let us know if you want raw pcap, but HAR should show headers.

Thanks,
Ravi

—
Ravi Desai
Senior Software Engineer
Northwind AI



From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ravi Desai <ravi.desai@northwind.ai>
Cc: Alyssa Kim <alyssa.kim@northwind.ai>, Monica Patel <monica@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 5, 2026 at 12:27 PM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients

Ravi/Alyssa — this is super helpful, thank you.

We can confirm quickly:
- Your HAR confirms the missing Retry-After header on that 429 path.
- The ~200–400ms loop aligns with our “no Retry-After + minimal delay” fallback in older SDK codepaths.

Immediate workaround (today):
- TS: set max_retries to 0 or 1 temporarily for your highest-volume services (especially those behind autoscalers that can amplify retry storms).
- Go: same guidance; until the patch, don’t rely on automatic 429 backoff.

We’re going to:
- Correlate those request_ids with platform logs.
- Prioritize rollout of the gateway change that guarantees Retry-After on 429.
- Get you the patch versions for TS/Go that (a) parse Retry-After reliably, and (b) apply exponential backoff w/ jitter + a hard cap.

We’ll send an ETA by EOD today.

Nikhil



From: Monica Patel <monica@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alyssa Kim <alyssa.kim@northwind.ai>, Ravi Desai <ravi.desai@northwind.ai>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 11:27 AM PST
Subject: Re: Escalation: 429 handling + retry storms from SDK clients

Alyssa/Ravi — follow-up with concrete next steps + timeline.

Platform (gateway):
- Ethan’s team is rolling out the change to always include Retry-After on 429 responses (including the endpoint path shown in your HAR). Target rollout: early next week, phased by region. This is tracked under ENG-5130 on our side.

SDK patches:
- TypeScript: we have a patch queued to (1) parse Retry-After in both seconds and HTTP-date format, and (2) enforce jittered exponential backoff with a max attempt cap across all request types (streaming + non-streaming). Target release: 2.18.1.
- Go: patch queued to respect Retry-After and avoid immediate retry loops when the header is missing; also aligns retryable-code classification. Target release: 0.9.5.

What we recommend you do until those are live:
1) Reduce 429 retries to 0–1 on your highest throughput services.
2) Add client-side throttling so you don’t rely on server-side 429 as your primary control loop.
3) Log request_id + retry_count per request so we can debug quickly if anything persists.

If you’re open to it, we can also add you to the beta channel once the SDK builds are ready (so you can validate in staging before production).

Monica

—
Monica Patel
Director, SDK Engineering
Redwood Inference

- Go: patch queued to respect Retry-After and avoid immediate retry loops when the header is missing; also aligns retryable-code classification. Target release: 0.9.5.

What we recommend you do until those are live:
1) Reduce 429 retries to 0–1 on your highest throughput services.
2) Add client-side throttling so you don’t rely on server-side 429 as your primary control loop.
3) Log request_id + retry_count per request so we can debug quickly if anything persists.

If you’re open to it, we can also add you to the beta channel once the SDK builds are ready (so you can validate in staging before production).

Monica

—
Monica Patel
Director, SDK Engineering
Redwood Inference
